proposals.title: Preference-Guided Quality-Diversity: Integrating Human Feedback for RL-Based Game Level Generation
proposals.motivation: Current RL-based procedural content generation (PCG) agents, such as those trained on benchmarks like the PCG Benchmark, optimize for automated metrics that often fail to capture nuanced human preferences for fun, challenge, and aesthetics. This leads to generated game content that is technically valid but may not be engaging for players. Integrating direct human judgment into the training loop is crucial for creating human-aligned generative AI for games.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating real-time human preference learning into a Quality-Diversity (QD) RL framework will produce a richer, more human-preferred diversity of game levels compared to optimization based solely on surrogate automated fitness functions.
proposals.method: We will extend the PCG Benchmark with a human-in-the-loop interface. An RL agent, using an algorithm like PPO or TD3, will generate levels for a game like MiniDungeons or a Mario-like platformer. The agent's objective will be shaped by a reward function combining: 1) traditional QD objectives (e.g., MAP-Elites) for behavioral diversity, and 2) a preference model trained via comparative feedback (e.g., Bradley-Terry model) from live human evaluators. Human feedback will be collected via pairwise comparisons of generated levels, actively querying for preferences on dimensions like 'fun' and 'fairness'.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, open-source framework for human-preferences-in-the-loop PCG, a new dataset of human preferences for generated game levels, and empirical evidence on the trade-offs between automated metrics and human judgment in guiding generative agents. It directly addresses the 'human' and 'competition' themes by aiming to generate content that better competes for human interest.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses human feedback to fine-tune generative models (e.g., RLHF for language), applying interactive preference learning specifically within a QD-RL loop for game level generation is novel. It moves beyond using human data only for evaluation or offline training, instead making human judgment a core, online component of the search-based generation process.

proposals.title: Generative Adversarial Game Design: Multi-Agent RL for Competitive and Collaborative Level Generation
proposals.motivation: Current procedural content generation (PCG) methods, including those in the PCG Benchmark, often treat level design as a monolithic optimization or sequence modeling task. This fails to capture the inherently multi-faceted nature of human design processes, which involve balancing competing objectives (e.g., challenge vs. flow, aesthetics vs. playability) and can be collaborative or adversarial. A multi-agent framework can model these dynamics explicitly, potentially leading to more diverse, balanced, and human-like game content.
proposals.hypothesis: Framing level generation as a multi-agent problem, where agents with distinct roles or objectives compete or collaborate, will produce levels that are more diverse, higher quality, and better at balancing competing design metrics than single-agent or monolithic generation systems.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) testbed within the open-source PCG Benchmark framework. For competitive design, we will implement a generative adversarial approach where a 'designer' agent (using a policy gradient method like PPO) builds levels to maximize a specific quality-diversity (QD) score, while a 'critic' agent (acting as an adversary) tries to identify flaws or predict human player frustration. For collaborative design, we will decompose the level into segments (e.g., combat arenas, traversal paths) and train specialized agents using MADDPG to co-design a cohesive whole, with a shared reward based on playability metrics from simulated agents.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel MARL-based framework for PCG that explicitly models design trade-offs, 2) new benchmark challenges within the PCG Benchmark for collaborative and adversarial generation, and 3) empirical analysis of how multi-agent dynamics affect the quality-diversity Pareto front of generated content, extending prior work on QD optimization.
proposals.novelty: While multi-agent RL has been used for playing games and GANs for level generation, applying MARL specifically to model the *process* of game design as a competitive or collaborative multi-agent system is a novel paradigm. It moves beyond single-policy generation to simulate a society of design intelligences.

proposals.title: Adversarial PCGRL: Generating Maximally Challenging Game Levels via Agent-in-the-Loop Optimization
proposals.motivation: Procedural Content Generation via Reinforcement Learning (PCGRL) has focused on generating playable, diverse, or human-like levels. However, a core purpose of game levels is to provide appropriate challenge. Pre-2026 benchmarks like the PCG Benchmark lack a formal framework for generating levels that are specifically tuned to be difficult for a given AI agent, which is crucial for automated playtesting, dynamic difficulty adjustment, and understanding agent capabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: By framing level generation as an adversarial optimization problem against a fixed solving agent, PCGRL can produce levels that are provably more challenging for that agent than those generated by standard, agent-agnostic methods, revealing specific agent weaknesses.
proposals.method: We will adapt the PCGRL framework, using a generator agent (e.g., a convolutional neural network) that receives rewards based on the failure of a separate, fixed solving agent. The solving agent's performance (e.g., time to solve, success rate) on a generated level becomes the fitness signal. We will employ Quality-Diversity (QD) algorithms like MAP-Elites to maintain a diverse archive of hard levels, preventing degenerate solutions and enabling analysis of challenge types. Experiments will use the PCG Benchmark's Sokoban or Zelda environments, with A* or pre-trained RL agents as solvers.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a formal benchmark task for adversarial level generation within the existing PCG Benchmark suite. It will provide a method for automated stress-testing of game-playing AI, identifying failure modes, and could inform the design of robust agents. The generated level archives will serve as a diagnostic tool for agent capability.
proposals.novelty: While PCGRL optimizes for solvability or style, directly inverting the objective to maximize difficulty for a specific agent is an underexplored dual problem. Combining this adversarial objective with QD optimization to curate a diverse 'challenge corpus' is a novel synthesis of pre-2026 methods.

proposals.title: Evolving Adaptive Agents for Live Games: A Quality-Diversity Approach to Continual Rule Changes
proposals.motivation: Modern online games frequently introduce new expansions, balance patches, and rule changes, creating a dynamic environment that breaks static AI agents. This necessitates AI that can adapt continually without full retraining. Pre-2026, while Quality-Diversity (QD) algorithms have shown promise for generating diverse, high-performing solutions, their application to continual adaptation in live, human-competitive game environments remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a QD-based population, maintained online and evolved with a novelty or surprise objective, will produce agents that can rapidly adapt to unforeseen game rule changes, outperforming fine-tuned monolithic agents in both performance and behavioral diversity after a change.
proposals.method: We will implement a testbed within the open-source Procedural Content Generation Benchmark (PCG-Bench) to simulate dynamic rule changes. We will use MAP-Elites as the core QD algorithm to maintain a population of agents (e.g., neural network controllers) in a behavior-performance map. Upon a rule change, we will employ a 'surprise' metric, calculated from the shift in agent performance or behavior characteristics, to drive rapid exploration of the new map. This will be compared to baselines of fine-tuning a single agent and population-based RL without explicit diversity maintenance.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel benchmark for continual adaptation in games within PCG-Bench, 2) a concrete method combining QD and surprise for online adaptation, and 3) empirical evidence on the trade-offs between adaptation speed, final performance, and behavioral diversity in dynamic environments, directly informing the design of human-like bots for live service games.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in framing game rule changes as a continual QD optimization problem, using in-game surprise as an explicit driver for exploration, rather than treating adaptation solely as a fine-tuning or catastrophic forgetting challenge in monolithic RL.

proposals.title: Generating Diverse and Competitive Game Levels via a League-Based Co-Evolutionary Framework
proposals.motivation: The success of AlphaStar's league training in StarCraft II demonstrated a powerful paradigm for generating diverse, robust agents through competitive co-evolution. However, this framework has not been systematically applied to the domain of procedural content generation (PCG), where generating levels that challenge a diverse set of player strategies remains an open problem. Applying this approach to PCG could create a self-improving ecosystem of levels and agents, directly addressing the need for adaptive, human-like challenge in games.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a competitive league-based training framework, adapted for co-evolution between level generators and playing agents, will produce a more diverse and strategically challenging set of game levels than static or single-population evolutionary methods.
proposals.method: We will adapt the AlphaStar league architecture to co-evolve a population of level generators (using latent variable models or conditional GANs) and a population of playing agents (using deep reinforcement learning). The league will maintain a pool of historical generators and agents, with new entrants trained via prioritized matchmaking against a mixture of current and past league members. The fitness for generators will be based on agent performance and diversity metrics, measured using a Quality-Diversity (QD) optimization benchmark. We will implement this framework within an open-source testbed, such as the PCG Benchmark, using a game with clear strategic depth (e.g., a simplified real-time strategy or puzzle game).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, generalizable co-evolutionary framework for PCG that explicitly optimizes for strategic diversity and adaptive difficulty. It will yield a new benchmark for evaluating generative systems in games based on their ability to challenge a spectrum of playstyles. The resulting open-source league system will provide a new tool for studying the dynamics of human-competitive gameplay and content generation.
proposals.novelty: While co-evolution and QD have been explored separately in PCG, the integration of a structured, historical league framework—inspired by multi-agent RL successes—to drive the generation of game content is a novel synthesis. This moves beyond optimizing for single objective functions and instead cultivates an entire ecosystem of content and play.

proposals.title: Explaining the Arena: Generating Human-Interpretable Strategy Reports from League Training Agents
proposals.motivation: League training, as seen in AlphaStar and OpenAI Five, produces superhuman agents with opaque, complex strategies. While these agents can be used as training partners or benchmarks, their value for human player education is limited without explanation. This work addresses the critical gap in eXplainable AI (XAI) for competitive games: translating an agent's evolved policy into actionable strategic insights for humans.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying quality-diversity (QD) optimization to a league of agents and analyzing the resulting diverse policy archive with feature attribution and natural language generation techniques, we can automatically produce human-understandable explanations of core strategic concepts and their trade-offs.
proposals.method: We will train a diverse league of agents in a real-time strategy testbed (e.g., based on the PCG Benchmark). We will then use MAP-Elites to create a structured archive of strategies, characterized by behavioral features (e.g., aggression, economy focus). For key cells in this archive, we will use saliency maps (e.g., based on integrated gradients) and trajectory analysis to identify decision-critical game states. A transformer-based model, prompted with these state-action pairs and feature descriptors, will generate concise natural language strategy summaries (e.g., 'This agent wins by securing early map control with cheap units').
proposals.contribution: This will yield a novel, automated pipeline for strategy explanation in complex games, directly linking agent training paradigms to human learning. We will contribute a new benchmark task for XAI in games, extending the PCG Benchmark, and release a dataset of paired agent policies and generated strategy explanations for community evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes or visualizes single-agent policies, this explicitly tackles the dual problem inherent in league training: explaining a population of diverse, competing strategies. It uniquely combines QD-based behavioral characterization with modern prompt-driven language models for explanation generation in a game-specific context.

proposals.title: The Adaptive Playground: Real-Time Player Experience Modeling for Dynamic Level Generation
proposals.motivation: Current procedural content generation (PCG) systems, as benchmarked in the PCG Benchmark, operate largely offline or with coarse, post-hoc player models. This limits their ability to create personalized, engaging experiences that respond to a player's immediate affective state and in-game behavior. Integrating real-time player experience modeling with level generation is a critical next step for creating truly adaptive games that can optimize for engagement, challenge, and flow on a per-player basis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a system combining real-time, sensor-based player affect estimation with quality-diversity (QD) optimization for level generation can produce dynamic level adaptations that measurably improve player engagement and perceived experience compared to static or reactive-difficulty PCG.
proposals.method: We will build upon the PCG Benchmark framework to create a new testbed for real-time adaptive generation. Player state will be modeled using compute-efficient sensors (e.g., input streams, in-game performance metrics) and lightweight affect recognition models, inspired by prior work on human-like bots. This real-time player model will serve as a dynamic feature vector for a QD algorithm (e.g., MAP-Elites). The algorithm will continuously search a space of level segments, using the player model to define behavioral niches (e.g., 'high-arousal-challenge', 'low-frustration-exploration') and a fitness function based on target engagement metrics, generating and swapping level components in real-time.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, open-source benchmark for real-time adaptive PCG, extending the existing PCG Benchmark; 2) a validated method for coupling real-time player modeling with QD-based generation; and 3) empirical evidence on the trade-offs between adaptation frequency, player experience, and generative stability.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight, real-time closed-loop integration of a continuous player experience model—derived from in-game sensors—as the driving objective for a quality-diversity optimization process applied to dynamic level generation, a synthesis not yet explored in pre-2026 PCG research.

proposals.title: Cross-Genre Generalization of Player Experience Models in Procedurally Generated Platformers
proposals.motivation: Player experience models (e.g., for fun, challenge, or frustration) are crucial for personalized and adaptive game generation, but they are typically trained and validated within a single game or a narrow genre. This limits their practical utility for generative systems designed to work across diverse game designs. As the field of procedural content generation via machine learning (PCGML) expands, establishing the robustness and generalizability of these models is a key pre-2026 challenge for creating truly adaptive game AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that player experience models trained on data from one sub-genre of platformer (e.g., precision-based 'Celeste-likes') will fail to generalize accurately to other sub-genres (e.g., exploration-based 'Metroidvanias'), and that a model incorporating cross-genre feature engineering or meta-learning will achieve superior generalization performance.
proposals.method: We will use the PCG Benchmark framework to generate levels from distinct platformer sub-genres, defined by specific level design constraints and mechanics. We will collect human playtraces and experience ratings for each genre. First, we will train baseline models (e.g., LSTMs or Transformers on player action sequences, combined with engineered spatial features from the level) on a single source genre and test on held-out data from other genres. We will then apply quality-diversity (QD) algorithms, like MAP-Elites, to optimize for a diverse set of level features that correlate with experience across genres, using these features to train a more robust model. Finally, we will explore few-shot meta-learning (e.g., MAML) to fine-tune models on new genres with minimal data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a benchmark for testing the cross-genre generalization of player models and a methodological framework for creating more robust models. It will provide concrete evidence on the transferability of experience models, a critical step towards general PCGML systems. The findings will directly inform the design of adaptive generators and human-like bots that can operate across different game environments.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has modeled player experience within a single game, systematically testing and improving model generalization across mechanically distinct sub-genres of the same broader genre is unexplored. The combination of the PCG Benchmark for controlled content generation with QD optimization for cross-genre feature discovery is a novel methodological synthesis for this problem.

proposals.title: Inferring Player Style from Level Design: A Benchmark for Inverse Procedural Content Generation
proposals.motivation: Procedural Content Generation (PCG) research, as exemplified by the PCG Benchmark, focuses on generating content from a specification. However, the inverse problem—inferring the intended player style or preferences from a given game level—remains underexplored. Solving this is crucial for creating adaptive games, personalized content, and tools that help designers understand the implicit biases in their levels. It bridges the gap between generative AI and human-centric game design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning model, trained on a dataset of levels annotated with target player archetypes or playstyle metrics, can accurately infer the intended player style from level design features. This mapping from level geometry and challenge placement to player preference is learnable and generalizable across similar game genres.
proposals.method: We will construct a benchmark dataset using the PCG Benchmark framework, generating levels for games like *Super Mario Bros.* and *Lode Runner* with known target player styles (e.g., 'explorer', 'speed-runner', 'completionist') defined via quality-diversity (QD) optimization or designer intent. We will extract spatial, topological, and difficulty features from levels. A model, likely a graph neural network or a vision transformer adapted for level topology, will be trained to predict the target style label. We will compare its performance against baselines using prompt-based feature extraction from large language models (pre-trained on game data) and simpler classifiers.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel benchmark task for inverse PCG, extending the PCG Benchmark's scope. It will provide a validated model and feature set for style inference, offering a new tool for automated game analysis and personalized content pipelines. The results will also yield insights into the relationship between level design elements and player experience.
proposals.novelty: While prior work generates levels *for* a style, we explicitly formalize and benchmark the reverse inference task. The approach integrates QD-based dataset creation, modern neural architectures for spatial data, and a human-centric evaluation grounded in player modeling, moving beyond pure content generation.

proposals.title: Dual: Adaptive Level Generation for Targeted Skill Acquisition Using Quality-Diversity and Player Modeling
proposals.motivation: Procedural content generation (PCG) has focused on creating varied or high-quality levels, but less on using levels as adaptive training tools. In game AI and human-computer interaction, personalized training that targets a player's specific weaknesses remains an open challenge. This work addresses the need for intelligent systems that can diagnose and remediate player skill deficits through tailored content, enhancing both player experience and AI training efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: Levels generated by a system that models a player's skill profile and uses quality-diversity (QD) optimization to explore the space of level features can more efficiently improve that player's performance in targeted weak areas compared to static or randomly generated levels.
proposals.method: We will extend the PCG Benchmark framework to include a player skill model, trained on in-game features (e.g., accuracy, resource management, path efficiency) from playtraces. A QD algorithm (e.g., MAP-Elites) will be used to generate a repertoire of levels characterized by skill-challenge dimensions. For a given player, the system will select or mutate levels from the repertoire that maximize a fitness function combining the player's skill gap and an engagement metric. The adaptation loop will be evaluated in a controlled testbed game like a simplified platformer or shooter.
proposals.contribution: This will contribute a novel, open-source framework for adaptive skill-based level generation, integrating player modeling with QD-PCG. It will provide empirical evidence on the efficacy of targeted PCG for human skill acquisition, creating a new benchmark task for generative AI in games. The work will also offer insights into the trade-offs between challenge, engagement, and learning rate.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses PCG for variety or difficulty adjustment, this proposal uniquely combines explicit, multi-dimensional skill modeling with QD-based generation to create a *dual* system that both diagnoses and treats specific gameplay weaknesses. It frames level generation not as an end in itself, but as a dynamic, closed-loop teaching tool.

proposals.title: Multi-Objective Quality-Diversity for Personalized Multiplayer Level Generation
proposals.motivation: Procedural content generation (PCG) has advanced in creating single-player personalized experiences, but multiplayer games present a critical, unsolved challenge. Balancing the diverse and often conflicting preferences of multiple simultaneous players in a cooperative or competitive level is essential for engagement and retention in modern games, yet existing PCG methods lack formal frameworks for this multi-agent, multi-objective optimization problem.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-objective Quality-Diversity (QD) algorithm, extended with a preference-conditioned generative model, can produce a diverse archive of game levels that effectively trade off between the quantified playstyle preferences of multiple players, leading to higher perceived fairness and enjoyment compared to non-personalized or single-player-personalized baselines.
proposals.method: We will extend the MAP-Elites QD algorithm to a multi-objective formulation where each objective corresponds to the predicted engagement of a specific player model. Player preferences will be modeled via behavioral features extracted from prior gameplay logs, similar to talent management classifiers. A conditional Variational Autoencoder (cVAE) or a diffusion model, prompted by a concatenated vector of player preferences, will serve as the generative model, with its latent space searched by the multi-objective QD algorithm. Evaluation will use the PCG Benchmark framework for computational assessment and human subject studies on competitive/cooperative testbeds.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel formalization and benchmark for multiplayer personalized PCG, 2) an open-source algorithm combining multi-objective QD with preference-conditioned deep generative models, and 3) empirical evidence on the trade-offs in multiplayer level design and their impact on human player experience.
proposals.novelty: While QD has been used for single-player PCG and multi-objective optimization is known, their integration for modeling *multiple, simultaneous human preferences* within a generative level design process is novel. This moves beyond aggregate difficulty balancing to personalized, multi-faceted design spaces.

proposals.title: Generalizing Across the Labyrinth: A Quality-Diversity and Foundation Model Approach to Robust Agent Training in Procedurally Generated Environments
proposals.motivation: Procedural content generation (PCG) is a cornerstone of modern game design, creating vast, replayable environments. However, current reinforcement learning (RL) agents often overfit to specific level instances, failing to generalize to unseen layouts, which severely limits their practical utility for robust game AI and simulation. This problem mirrors a core challenge in AI: achieving robust, human-like generalization from limited, varied experience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training agents on a quality-diverse (QD) spectrum of procedurally generated levels, while using foundation model-derived semantic features to guide exploration and representation learning, will produce agents with superior generalization to novel, unseen floor layouts compared to standard RL or domain randomization.
proposals.method: We will leverage the PCG Benchmark framework to generate training and test sets of diverse floor layouts (e.g., from roguelike games). We will employ a two-stage approach: first, using QD optimization (e.g., MAP-Elites) to create a diverse archive of level-feature pairs and corresponding agent policies. Second, we will integrate a vision-language foundation model (e.g., CLIP) to extract semantic, human-interpretable features from level screenshots, using these features to condition a policy network (via prompt-like embeddings) and to structure the QD behavioral descriptor space, forcing the agent to learn adaptable strategies based on core environmental concepts.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, hybrid QD-foundation model training methodology for robust agent generalization, 2) a new benchmark and set of evaluation metrics within the PCG Benchmark for assessing cross-level generalization, and 3) open-source agents that demonstrate human-like adaptability in unseen procedural environments, advancing both game AI and sim-to-real research.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit combination of QD optimization for generating strategic training curricula with foundation model-derived semantic features to guide agent representation learning, moving beyond raw pixel or tile inputs to learn from human-like conceptual abstractions of the environment.

proposals.title: The Imperfect Information Challenge: Benchmarking Agent Adaptation from Perfect to Partial Game State Observations
proposals.motivation: Most AI game-playing agents are developed and benchmarked under the unrealistic assumption of perfect, low-latency access to the full game state. This creates a significant sim-to-real gap for deploying agents in real-world scenarios, such as competitive esports or human-AI collaboration, where sensory input is inherently partial, noisy, and delayed. Addressing this gap is crucial for developing robust, human-like AI that can operate under realistic constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that agents trained initially with perfect state information can be effectively adapted to perform robustly under partial, noisy, and delayed observations by fine-tuning with a curriculum of progressively degraded sensory inputs, and that this adaptation process reveals fundamental trade-offs between performance, robustness, and computational efficiency.
proposals.method: We will extend the Procedural Content Generation Benchmark (PCG-Bench) framework to include configurable sensory degradation modules (e.g., occlusion, noise injection, action-observation delays). We will train state-of-the-art reinforcement learning agents (e.g., PPO, Rainbow DQN) on selected benchmarks with perfect state access, then apply a Quality-Diversity (QD) optimization approach, specifically MAP-Elites, to generate a diverse population of agents fine-tuned across a spectrum of sensory conditions. The agents' 'features' for QD will be their performance profiles under different degradation types.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, open-source benchmark extension for evaluating agent robustness to imperfect information, 2) a systematic analysis of adaptation strategies and their trade-offs, and 3) new 'human-like' agent models for tactical shooters and other genres that are more viable for real-time competition due to their use of compute-efficient, degraded sensors.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied partial observability in isolation, the novelty lies in explicitly benchmarking the *adaptation pathway* from perfect to imperfect state, using QD optimization to map the performance landscape and generate specialized, robust agents, rather than seeking a single generalist policy.

proposals.title: The PCG Benchmark for Sample-Efficient Agent Training: Learning from Limited Interactions via Procedural Content Generation
proposals.motivation: While modern AI agents can master games like Go or StarCraft, they require millions or billions of costly environment interactions. This is infeasible for many real-world applications, including game testing and robotics, where simulation is expensive. We need agents that learn efficiently from a severely limited interaction budget, a core challenge in sample-efficient reinforcement learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an agent trained on a diverse, procedurally generated curriculum of game levels, generated and selected using Quality-Diversity (QD) algorithms, will achieve higher performance with a fixed, small interaction budget than an agent trained on a static or randomly generated set of levels.
proposals.method: We will extend the open-source Procedural Content Generation Benchmark (PCGB) to include sample efficiency as a core metric. We will develop a co-optimization loop where a QD algorithm (e.g., MAP-Elites) generates levels that maximize a diversity metric (e.g., game feature space) and an agent performance metric. A sample-efficient RL agent (e.g., using model-based RL like DreamerV2 or data augmentation) will be trained on a small, dynamically curated set of these levels. The benchmark will compare this approach against baselines on fixed and random level sets.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel benchmark for sample-efficient RL within the established PCGB framework, 2) a concrete methodology combining QD-based level generation with sample-efficient RL, and 3) empirical evidence on how tailored environmental diversity can drastically reduce the data requirements for learning competent game-playing agents.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly using PCG and QD not for generating final content, but as an active learning mechanism to create an optimal, minimal training curriculum for sample-starved agents, a perspective not deeply explored in pre-2026 PCG or RL research.

proposals.title: Generating Adaptive Human-Like Opponents via Quality-Diversity and Behavioral Prompting for Multi-Agent Learning
proposals.motivation: Current multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) benchmarks often use static or simplistic opponents, failing to capture the adaptive, human-like competition crucial for robust AI. This limits the transfer of learned policies to real-world scenarios involving human players. A principled framework for generating diverse, adaptive, and human-like opponent populations is needed to create more challenging and ecologically valid training environments.
proposals.hypothesis: That a hybrid approach combining Quality-Diversity (QD) optimization for behavioral diversity with language model-based behavioral prompting can generate a population of adaptive, human-like opponents that more effectively stress-test and improve the robustness of single-agent learners in complex multi-agent environments.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage pipeline within an open-source game testbed like the PCG Benchmark. First, we will use MAP-Elites to generate a diverse archive of opponent behavior descriptors (e.g., aggression, exploration, teamwork) and corresponding policy parameters. Second, we will use a large language model (e.g., GPT-4), prompted with descriptions of human play styles derived from game telemetry, to seed or guide the QD search towards regions of the behavior space characteristic of human players. The resulting opponent population will be used to train and evaluate a single-agent learner.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, open-source benchmark for adaptive opponent generation, extending the PCG Benchmark; 2) a new hybrid QD-prompting method for creating human-like agent behavior; 3) empirical analysis of how training against such adaptive populations affects the robustness and generalization of single-agent policies in competitive games.
proposals.novelty: While QD has been used for generating game content and bots, its integration with LLM behavioral prompting to explicitly shape an opponent population towards human-like adaptability is novel. This moves beyond optimizing for a single objective (e.g., win rate) to curating a diverse, human-relevant challenge space.

proposals.title: Learning to Play by Assuming the Goal: Intrinsic Reward Generation for Human-Like Gameplay
proposals.motivation: Current reinforcement learning agents in games rely on precise, hand-crafted reward functions, which are expensive to design and often misaligned with desirable, human-like behavior. This limits their adaptability and deployment in complex, open-ended game environments where the 'goal' is implicit or multifaceted. Developing agents that can infer their own reward signals is a critical step towards more general and autonomous game AI.
proposals.hypothesis: An agent can learn to generate its own intrinsic reward function by assuming that its observed initial state transitions and sparse terminal signals (like win/loss) implicitly define a goal, and that optimizing for this self-inferred reward will produce more robust and human-like behavior than optimizing for the game's extrinsic score.
proposals.method: We will use a two-phase approach within the PCG Benchmark framework. First, a transformer-based world model will be trained on initial gameplay traces (agent or human) to predict state transitions and a learned latent reward. Second, an agent will be trained via PPO or an evolutionary strategy (leveraging quality-diversity methods) to maximize this learned reward, with regularization to maintain policy diversity. The agent's behavior will be evaluated against human gameplay features and benchmark scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, practical method for reward inference that reduces reliance on detailed reward shaping. It will provide new benchmarks within the PCG Benchmark for evaluating agents on learned reward robustness and human-likeness, advancing the field of autonomous agent design for games.
proposals.novelty: While intrinsic motivation exists, this approach uniquely frames the initial game dynamics and sparse outcomes as a prompt from which to generate a complete reward function, blending world modeling and quality-diversity optimization in a novel way for game AI.

proposals.title: Benchmarking and Improving Sample-Efficient End-to-End Learning from Pixels in Complex 3D Games
proposals.motivation: End-to-end learning from raw pixels is a grand challenge in AI, with games serving as ideal testbeds. However, progress in complex 3D environments (e.g., tactical shooters, racing games) has been hampered by extreme sample inefficiency and the difficulty of learning meaningful representations from high-dimensional visual input. A systematic benchmark for this specific challenge is lacking, hindering reproducible progress and fair comparison of methods.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a novel benchmark built on the PCG Benchmark framework, combined with a hybrid architecture integrating world models and quality-diversity (QD) optimization, will significantly improve the sample efficiency and final performance of agents learning directly from pixels in complex 3D games.
proposals.method: We will extend the open-source Procedural Content Generation (PCG) Benchmark to include a new challenge track for end-to-end pixel-based learning in procedurally generated 3D levels from games like Minecraft or Doom. The core agent architecture will combine a convolutional encoder with a recurrent state-space model (RSSM) to learn a compact latent dynamics model. We will then use MAP-Elites, a QD algorithm, to explicitly optimize for a diversity of high-performing latent skill representations, encouraging robust and generalizable policy learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a new, open-source benchmark for reproducible research on pixel-based learning in complex 3D games, 2) a novel hybrid agent architecture demonstrating state-of-the-art sample efficiency on this benchmark, and 3) an analysis of the trade-offs between representation learning fidelity and behavioral diversity for end-to-end control.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of QD optimization—typically used for content generation—into the latent space of a world model for pixel-based control, and the formalization of this problem within a standardized, procedurally varied benchmark to measure generalization.

proposals.title: Generating Human-Competitive Game Agents via Abstract State Representations and Quality-Diversity Optimization
proposals.motivation: Current AI for games often relies on raw pixels or low-level features, which is computationally expensive and diverges from how human players perceive and reason about game state. This creates a barrier to developing agents that compete fairly and understandably with humans, and limits the scalability of agent generation across diverse game levels. A shift towards abstract, semantically rich state representations could bridge this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: Agents trained on abstract, human-interpretable game state representations (e.g., entity relationships, strategic affordances) will achieve more human-like competitive performance and exhibit more robust generalization across procedurally generated levels than agents trained on raw sensory inputs.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework that uses large language models (LLMs) as 'state abstractors,' prompted to convert raw game observations into structured JSON descriptions of entities, their properties, and relationships. This abstract state will serve as input to a policy network. We will train and evaluate agents using the PCG Benchmark, employing MAP-Elites for Quality-Diversity optimization to generate a repertoire of agents specialized for different level features, directly optimizing for both performance and behavioral metrics derived from the abstract state.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodology for building more efficient and human-like game AI, directly contributing to the PCG Benchmark with new agent generation challenges. It will also yield insights into how abstract state representations affect the trade-offs between agent performance, generalization, and behavioral diversity.
proposals.novelty: While LLMs have been used for game playing, their use as a core perceptual module to produce structured, abstract state for a downstream policy, combined with QD optimization for generating a diverse population of such agents, is a novel synthesis. This moves beyond using LLMs for end-to-end control or narrative generation.

proposals.title: Beyond the Scoreboard: A Quality-Diversity Framework for Generating Novel and Diverse Game AI
proposals.motivation: Current AI for games is overwhelmingly optimized for pure performance, leading to predictable, homogeneous strategies that are brittle and uninteresting for human players. This narrow focus neglects the rich space of possible playstyles and fails to generate AI that can provide varied, engaging, and human-like competition. The field needs a principled shift from optimizing a single fitness to illuminating a behavioral space.
proposals.hypothesis: By using Quality-Diversity (QD) algorithms, specifically MAP-Elites, to optimize for behavioral diversity alongside a minimal performance constraint, we can generate a repertoire of game-playing agents that are more novel, strategically diverse, and engaging for human players than agents optimized solely for win-rate.
proposals.method: We will implement this within the open-source Procedural Content Generation Benchmark (PCG-Benchmark) framework. For a chosen game (e.g., a real-time strategy or shooter testbed), we will define a low-dimensional behavioral descriptor space (e.g., aggression, resource allocation, map control) and a minimal viability fitness (e.g., not losing instantly). Using MAP-Elites, we will populate an archive of agents, each excelling in a unique region of this behavioral space. We will compare this archive's agents against traditional fitness-maximizing (e.g., neuroevolution) and prompt-based LLM agents in benchmark tournaments and human-subject studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a concrete methodology and benchmark for evaluating AI diversity in games, 2) an open-source archive of diverse, viable game AI agents, and 3) empirical evidence on the trade-offs between performance, novelty, and human engagement in competitive AI. It directly extends the PCG-Benchmark's philosophy to AI generation.
proposals.novelty: While QD is used for content generation and talent management, its application to directly generate a diverse population of *game-playing agents*—explicitly decoupling behavioral novelty from raw performance—is novel. It challenges the core assumption that the objective is a single 'best' AI.

proposals.title: Generating Diverse Playstyles in Open-Ended Games via Human-Guided Quality-Diversity Optimization
proposals.motivation: Procedural content generation (PCG) and agent training for open-ended games (e.g., Minecraft, The Sims) are hindered by the lack of clear win/loss conditions, making fitness function design a critical bottleneck. Current methods often rely on simplistic, domain-specific heuristics that fail to capture the richness of human play and the multi-faceted notion of 'interesting' content. This limits the applicability of automated generation and benchmarking frameworks in these complex, player-centric domains.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid fitness function, combining human preference models via preference learning with automated feature discovery from gameplay traces, can more effectively guide Quality-Diversity (QD) algorithms to generate a wider range of engaging and human-like content and behaviors in open-ended games.
proposals.method: We will extend the PCG Benchmark framework to support open-ended games. Our core method integrates: 1) A preference model trained on human rankings of gameplay clips or generated levels, using techniques like Duelling Network Regressors. 2) An automated feature extractor (e.g., a CNN or autoencoder) to derive behavioral descriptors from gameplay states, forming the diversity dimensions for a QD algorithm like MAP-Elites. 3) The QD algorithm's objective will be optimized using a composite fitness function weighting the human preference score and novelty relative to the feature space. We will test this in a sandbox environment like a simplified Minecraft terrain generation task.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, generalizable framework for fitness evaluation in open-ended PCG and agent training, moving beyond win-rate metrics. It will yield a new benchmark challenge for the community and advance methods for creating human-aligned, diverse game content. The resulting hybrid QD approach will demonstrate how to balance human judgment with automated exploration.
proposals.novelty: While QD and preference learning exist separately, their integration for open-ended game evaluation is novel. This moves beyond using human data solely for final evaluation or training monolithic agents, instead embedding human-like taste directly into the generative search process to shape a diverse archive of solutions.

proposals.title: Evolving Ultra-Lightweight Neural Networks for Real-Time Game AI: A Quality-Diversity Approach to Mobile PCG Benchmarking
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of mobile gaming demands AI that can run in real-time on resource-constrained devices, yet most game AI research focuses on large, computationally expensive models. Simultaneously, the Procedural Content Generation (PCG) community lacks standardized benchmarks for evaluating generative models under strict computational budgets, a gap highlighted by the recent PCG Benchmark framework. This work addresses both by creating a testbed for evolving extremely small networks that generate or control game content on mobile hardware.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying Quality-Diversity (QD) optimization—specifically MAP-Elites—to the evolution of ultra-lightweight neural networks (e.g., <10k parameters), we can discover a diverse repertoire of effective, real-time game AI agents or content generators that outperform hand-designed heuristics and fit within mobile compute constraints.
proposals.method: We will extend the open-source PCG Benchmark to include a new challenge track for mobile-scale networks. Using a neuroevolution approach, we will evolve Tiny Neural Networks (TNNs) with constrained architectures (e.g., few layers, limited neurons). MAP-Elites will be employed to illuminate trade-offs in the behavioral space (e.g., aggression vs. defensiveness in a simple game) while maintaining real-time performance. The fitness function will combine task performance (e.g., game score) with a strict latency penalty measured on a target mobile processor emulator.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, open benchmark for PCG and game AI under severe computational limits, 2) a publicly available repertoire of evolved, ultra-lightweight networks for mobile game AI, and 3) an analysis of the trade-offs between network size, behavioral diversity, and performance in real-time game environments.
proposals.novelty: While QD has been used for game AI and PCG, its application to the co-evolution of network topology and weights for *ultra-lightweight* models targeting mobile real-time constraints is novel. This bridges Togelius's work on benchmarks, human-like bots with efficient sensors, and QD for managing trade-offs.

proposals.title: Co-Evolving AI Opponents and Player Interfaces for Dynamic Human-AI Competition
proposals.motivation: Current research in AI for games focuses on evolving agents to beat humans within fixed game environments, often leading to superhuman but incomprehensible or frustrating opponents. This neglects the potential for the game itself, particularly the human interface and mechanics, to adaptively change to maintain engaging and fair competition. Grounded in pre-2026 PCG and quality-diversity (QD) research, this work addresses how to sustain long-term human engagement with AI opponents.
proposals.hypothesis: Simultaneously evolving AI agent behavior and interpretable human interface parameters using a co-evolutionary or QD framework will produce more engaging and sustainable human-AI competitive ecosystems than optimizing the AI alone.
proposals.method: We will build upon the PCG Benchmark and MAP-Elites QD algorithm. A two-population co-evolutionary system will be implemented: one population of AI agents (e.g., neural network controllers) and one population of game 'modifiers' (e.g., parameters affecting player weapon strength, visibility fog, or resource spawn rates). These populations will compete in a testbed game, like a simplified tactical shooter. Fitness for AI is winning probability; fitness for modifiers is a multi-objective measure of game balance, engagement (derived from playtraces), and human interpretability. The system will be evaluated using human subject studies via a benchmark platform.
proposals.contribution: This will yield: 1) a novel methodological framework for co-evolving interactive systems, 2) new benchmark challenges within the PCG Benchmark for human-in-the-loop evolution, and 3) empirical evidence on what types of adaptive mechanics sustain human engagement against evolving AI. It directly extends Togelius's work on benchmarks, QD, and human-like bots.
proposals.novelty: While AI and content generation are often evolved separately, this proposal explicitly couples the evolution of the AI opponent with the evolution of the human player's interactive affordances, treating the competitive interface as a mutable component to be optimized for human experience.

proposals.title: MAP-Elites for Micro-Adaptation: Real-Time Quality-Diversity Generation of Game Levels During Play
proposals.motivation: Procedural Content Generation (PCG) is a cornerstone of modern game design, but current systems are largely offline, generating content before play. This limits a game's ability to dynamically respond to a player's skill, engagement, or playstyle in real-time. Real-time PCG, especially for level segments, could enable unprecedented personalization and replayability, but it requires algorithms that are both fast and capable of generating a diverse, high-quality set of content options on demand.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a fast, pre-trained Quality-Diversity (QD) algorithm, specifically a MAP-Elites grid, can generate novel, playable, and stylistically varied game level chunks in real-time (under 100ms) in response to live player metrics, thereby improving player engagement compared to static or simple random generation.
proposals.method: We will extend the PCG Benchmark framework to support a real-time generation API. A MAP-Elites algorithm will be trained offline on a corpus of level segments, using behavioral descriptors (e.g., difficulty, exploration density, resource scarcity) and a quality metric (e.g., playability via an A* agent). During live gameplay, a lightweight player model will map current player state (e.g., health, time, actions) to a target cell in the pre-computed MAP-Elites grid, triggering the instant retrieval and insertion of a corresponding level segment. We will test this in a 2D platformer testbed, comparing against static and purely random generation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first concrete framework and benchmark for real-time QD-PCG, a novel subfield. It will yield a publicly available testbed module for the PCG Benchmark and empirical data on the feasibility and player impact of micro-adaptive level generation. This bridges the gap between offline generative AI and real-time game adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While QD has been used for PCG and real-time adaptation has been explored with simpler methods, the application of a pre-trained MAP-Elites archive for instantaneous, diversity-driven content generation *during* live gameplay is a novel synthesis. It moves QD from a design-time tool to a runtime game engine component.

proposals.title: Generating Minimal Viable Game Designs: A Quality-Diversity Approach to Core Mechanic Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Procedural Content Generation (PCG) often focuses on expansive content like levels or assets, but the automated design of a game's core mechanics remains a significant challenge. Identifying the minimal set of mechanics that yield diverse and engaging gameplay is crucial for rapid prototyping, educational tools, and understanding fundamental game design principles. This problem aligns with the need for more generative and benchmark-driven research in game AI.
proposals.hypothesis: Quality-Diversity (QD) algorithms, such as MAP-Elites, can be effectively adapted to search a space of possible game mechanics to discover Pareto-optimal sets that maximize gameplay diversity while minimizing mechanical complexity.
proposals.method: We will define a search space where a genome encodes potential game mechanics (e.g., movement rules, interaction types, win conditions) for a simple 2D grid-world game framework. A QD algorithm, specifically MAP-Elites, will be employed. The 'quality' objective will be a composite score of player engagement and functional coherence, while the 'behavioral descriptor' dimensions will quantify emergent gameplay diversity using features from agent playtraces (e.g., entropy of action sequences, spatial coverage). The search will explicitly reward designs that achieve high descriptor coverage with fewer active mechanics.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, automated method for minimalist game design exploration and a new benchmark task for generative AI in games, extending the PCG Benchmark framework. It will also provide empirical insights into the trade-offs between design simplicity and experiential richness, a core concern in human-centric game design.
proposals.novelty: While QD has been used for level and artifact generation, applying it to synthesize the fundamental rule sets of games—treating the game's core logic as the content to be generated—is a novel direction. This shifts the generative target from content within a game to the generative parameters of the game itself.

proposals.title: Model-Free Deep Reinforcement Learning for General Video Game AI: Benchmarking and Analysis in the GVGAI Framework
proposals.motivation: The General Video Game AI (GVGAI) framework has been a key benchmark for planning and search-based agents, often relying on access to a forward model for simulation. However, this is unrealistic for many real-world game-playing scenarios, such as commercial video games, where the internal dynamics are unknown. Developing and benchmarking Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) agents that learn purely from pixels and scores, without a forward model, is crucial for advancing agents that can operate in more general, human-like conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modern, sample-efficient DRL algorithms, when combined with appropriate neural architectures for visual input, can achieve competitive performance in a diverse subset of GVGAI games without forward model access, and that their failure modes will systematically differ from planning-based agents.
proposals.method: We will implement and benchmark several pre-2026 DRL algorithms (e.g., Rainbow DQN, PPO, and IMPALA) within the GVGAI framework, using the raw game screen as input. The agent's convolutional neural network will be tasked with processing the visual state and predicting actions. Performance will be evaluated on a curated set of GVGAI games from the existing benchmark, comparing scores and learning efficiency against state-of-the-art planning agents (where possible) and reporting results in the established Procedural Content Generation Benchmark format to ensure reproducibility.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic benchmark of model-free DRL agents across the diverse GVGAI game set, establishing a new performance baseline for learning-based approaches. It will contribute a new, open-source agent framework to the GVGAI community and generate insights into which game features (e.g., puzzle logic, real-time action) are most challenging for model-free learning.
proposals.novelty: While DRL has been applied to individual games like Atari, its systematic evaluation and comparison against planning paradigms within the structured, multi-game GVGAI benchmark—explicitly without a forward model—is novel. The work directly contrasts learning-from-experience with classical search, a core tension in game AI.

proposals.title: Generalization from Human-Crafted Content: Benchmarking Agent Robustness on Procedurally Generated Levels
proposals.motivation: Current AI for games is often trained and tested on procedurally generated levels, assuming this ensures robustness. However, this overlooks a critical real-world scenario: agents are typically trained on a finite set of human-designed levels (e.g., a game's official campaign) but must perform reliably in user-generated or procedural content post-release. This gap creates a risk of overfitting to the quirks of specific generators rather than learning generalizable game-playing skills.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that agents trained on a curated set of high-quality human-designed levels will demonstrate superior generalization to novel procedural content, compared to agents trained directly on procedural content, by learning more fundamental, transferable game mechanics and strategies.
proposals.method: We will use the PCG Benchmark framework to define a controlled experiment in a tile-based game (e.g., MiniDungeons or a Mario-like platformer). A state-of-the-art RL agent (e.g., PPO or an evolution strategy) will be trained on a fixed corpus of human-designed levels. Its generalization will be tested against a suite of procedural generators (e.g., using Markov chains, grammars, or noise-based methods) from the benchmark. We will analyze performance drops and use Quality-Diversity (QD) algorithms, like MAP-Elites, to characterize the failure modes and feature spaces where generalization breaks down.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel benchmark for evaluating generalization in game AI, shifting the focus from pure performance to robustness. It will provide empirical evidence on the trade-offs between training on human vs. procedural content and yield insights into what makes a level corpus effective for training generalist agents.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard PCGML paradigm of learning from procedural data to generate human-like content, instead using human content as the training source for robust AI. It formally tests the often-assumed but rarely validated claim that human levels teach more generalizable skills.

proposals.title: Active Curriculum Learning: Querying Generative Level Design for Maximized Agent Competence
proposals.motivation: Training generalist game-playing agents requires exposure to a diverse and challenging set of levels, but manually designing such curricula is infeasible at scale. While procedural content generation (PCG) can produce vast level sets, randomly sampling from a generator is inefficient for learning. An automated method is needed to actively select training levels that most effectively improve an agent's performance, closing the loop between content generation and agent learning.
proposals.hypothesis: An active learning loop, which queries a PCG system for levels predicted to maximize an agent's immediate learning progress, will train more competent and sample-efficient agents compared to training on randomly generated or hand-designed curricula.
proposals.method: We will implement an active learning framework where a reinforcement learning agent (e.g., PPO) interacts with a PCG generator (e.g., a GAN or variational autoencoder trained on game levels). A surrogate model (e.g., a Gaussian Process) will predict the learning progress (improvement in expected return) for candidate levels based on agent-centric features (e.g., difficulty, novelty). This model will guide the querying of the generator via quality-diversity or Bayesian optimization to produce the most informative training level at each curriculum step, as benchmarked within the PCG Benchmark framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, automated curriculum generation algorithm that tightly couples PCG and RL. It will provide empirical evidence on how level selection impacts agent generalization and sample efficiency, offering a new benchmark task for adaptive content generation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses PCG for RL or static curricula, this actively queries a generator in real-time based on a direct, model-based prediction of learning progress, creating a dynamic, closed-loop training environment.

proposals.title: Massively Parallel Co-Evolution of Levels and Agents for Robust Policy Discovery
proposals.motivation: Current AI training often relies on static or hand-crafted level sets, which can lead to policies that overfit and fail to generalize. Co-evolution of levels and agents is a promising alternative for generating adaptive curricula, but it has been limited by computational cost and small population sizes. Scaling this process massively is necessary to discover truly robust agents and a diverse, challenging frontier of training content.
proposals.hypothesis: Massively parallel co-evolution, leveraging cloud-scale compute, will produce agents with significantly greater robustness and generalization capability compared to those trained on static curricula, while simultaneously generating a broad, high-quality benchmark of levels that expose distinct failure modes.
proposals.method: We will implement a large-scale asynchronous evolutionary framework using Quality-Diversity (QD) algorithms like MAP-Elites. One population will evolve levels (using generative models or direct encoding) for difficulty and stylistic features, while a separate population evolves agent policies (e.g., via RL or neuroevolution). The two populations will interact in a scalable, tournament-style evaluation system, inspired by the PCG Benchmark, running on thousands of parallel instances. Agent fitness will be based on performance across a diverse set of co-evolved levels, and level fitness on their ability to challenge a diverse set of co-evolved agents.
proposals.contribution: 1) A scalable, open-source framework for large-scale level-agent co-evolution. 2) New benchmarks of robust agents and challenging, diverse levels for games like MiniGrid or simple platformers. 3) Empirical analysis of the trade-offs between robustness, specialization, and curriculum progression in co-evolutionary systems.
proposals.novelty: While co-evolution is known, the application of massive parallelism and QD principles to simultaneously explore the spaces of both content and policy at this scale is unexplored. This moves beyond small-scale proof-of-concepts towards a practical, high-throughput engine for generating both robust AI and its evaluation suite.

proposals.title: The General Game Mechanic Challenge: A Benchmark and Agent Framework for Cross-Mechanic Generalization
proposals.motivation: Current PCG and AI research often focuses on generating or mastering content within a single game's fixed rule set, such as new levels for Super Mario Bros. This fails to capture the core challenge of general game intelligence: understanding and adapting to novel, high-level mechanics. A system that can generalize across fundamentally different game rules would represent a significant step towards more robust and creative AI, with applications in automated game design, adaptive tutoring systems, and more general problem-solving agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by training on a structured curriculum of games with progressively varied core mechanics, an agent equipped with a modular policy architecture and a mechanic-aware latent representation can learn transferable skills that enable zero-shot performance on games with unseen, but related, mechanical concepts.
proposals.method: We will extend the PCG Benchmark framework to create a new testbed of simple 2D games (e.g., variants of Griddly, OpenAI Gym Retro) where core mechanics (e.g., 'key-door', 'push-block', 'collect-while-avoiding') are explicitly parameterized and can be recombined. We will train agents using a combination of reinforcement learning (PPO) and quality-diversity (MAP-Elites) to develop a repertoire of behaviors. A key component is a 'mechanic prompt' encoder, inspired by language-conditioned policies, that ingests a formal description of the game's mechanics to modulate the agent's policy network, forcing it to separate mechanic-specific knowledge from general spatial reasoning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a new open-source benchmark for evaluating cross-mechanic generalization in games, 2) a novel agent architecture that explicitly conditions on game mechanics, and 3) empirical results demonstrating the feasibility and limits of zero-shot transfer between games with different rules, moving beyond level-based generalization.
proposals.novelty: While prior work benchmarks level or texture generalization, and some research explores transfer between distinct games, this proposal systematically isolates and benchmarks generalization across core game *mechanics* as a first-class objective, using a curriculum and a structured 'mechanic prompt' to guide the learning process.

proposals.title: Generating Game Levels from Non-Game Data: A Benchmark for Data-Efficient PCG
proposals.motivation: Current PCG via machine learning often relies on large, domain-specific datasets (e.g., thousands of Super Mario Bros. levels), which are costly to create and limit applicability to new or niche games. This creates a barrier to entry and contradicts the generalist aspirations of generative AI. Exploring data-efficient methods is crucial for more accessible and adaptable game AI.
proposals.hypothesis: Generative models, particularly those using quality-diversity (QD) optimization or few-shot prompting of pre-trained vision-language models, can produce diverse, playable, and human-like game levels when trained on very small game-specific datasets or even on non-game spatial/temporal data (e.g., architectural floor plans, abstract art).
proposals.method: We will use the PCG Benchmark framework to test this. First, we will train small VAEs or GANs on minimal game data (e.g., 50 levels) and use MAP-Elites for QD optimization to expand diversity. Second, we will fine-tune a pre-trained vision transformer (like ViT) or a diffusion model on non-game spatial datasets, then use prompt-based steering (e.g., CLIP-guided generation) to adapt outputs to target game mechanics. All methods will be evaluated on standard PCG metrics (diversity, playability, fun) and compared to large-dataset baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic analysis of data-efficient PCG, yielding concrete guidelines for when small or out-of-domain data suffices. It will extend the PCG Benchmark with new challenges focused on data scarcity and transfer learning, providing a vital testbed for future research in low-resource generative AI for games.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses large game datasets or generic QD, systematically benchmarking the sufficiency of non-game data for PCG is unexplored. The combination of few-shot learning, cross-domain transfer, and rigorous benchmarking within a established testbed is a novel synthesis.

proposals.title: Automated Self-Critique for Game Content Generation: Closing the Loop with Quality-Diversity and Learned Evaluation
proposals.motivation: Current procedural content generation (PCG) systems often rely on a human-in-the-loop to evaluate and steer the creative process, which is a bottleneck for scale and autonomy. This limits the development of truly adaptive, personalized, or massively content-driven games. An AI that can autonomously critique and improve its own output would represent a major step towards autonomous game design and more robust generative systems.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a trainable, multi-faceted critique module—modeled on human designer preferences and game-specific benchmarks—with a quality-diversity (QD) optimization loop, a PCG system can iteratively self-improve its content without human intervention, surpassing the quality and diversity of open-loop generation.
proposals.method: We will build on the PCG Benchmark framework to establish a testbed. The system will pair a generator (e.g., a diffusion model or CPPN) with a learned critic model trained on human preference data and analytic game features (e.g., difficulty, linearity, novelty). This critic will provide multi-objective feedback to a QD algorithm (like MAP-Elites) to populate an archive of high-quality, diverse content. The critic will be periodically retrained on the elite archive, creating a self-improving cycle.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel, closed-loop PCG architecture demonstrating autonomous quality refinement. 2) A new benchmark challenge within the PCG Benchmark suite for self-improving generative systems. 3) Empirical analysis of the trade-offs between automated critique fidelity and content diversity, extending prior work on classifier bias in QD.
proposals.novelty: While learned evaluation functions exist, fully closing the loop by using the critic to drive QD optimization and then retraining the critic on its own elite outputs creates a self-referential improvement cycle not yet explored in PCG. This inverts the typical paradigm where the human provides the final, static evaluation function.

proposals.title: Hybrid PCG via Latent Space Illumination: Combining Diffusion Models and MAP-Elites for Controllable Level Generation
proposals.motivation: Deep generative models for PCG, such as diffusion models, can produce high-quality, human-like content but offer limited control over specific level features. Conversely, search-based methods like Quality-Diversity (QD) optimization provide fine-grained control and diversity but often struggle with the raw quality and coherence of complex artifacts. A hybrid approach is needed to achieve both high fidelity and precise controllability, a key challenge in automated game design.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a diffusion model as a generative prior within a MAP-Elites search framework operating on the model's latent space, we can generate game levels that are both high in perceptual quality and systematically diverse according to target designer-specified feature dimensions.
proposals.method: We will train a latent diffusion model on a corpus of human-designed game levels (e.g., from the Mario AI Framework or the PCG Benchmark). A MAP-Elites algorithm will then search the model's latent space, using the diffusion decoder to produce candidate levels. The search will be guided by a fitness function (e.g., playability via an A* agent) and behavior descriptors (e.g., linearity, enemy density, leniency). The QD archive will store high-performing, diverse latent vectors, which can be decoded on-demand.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, open-source hybrid PCG algorithm and a benchmark for controllable generation within the PCG Benchmark testbed. It will provide empirical evidence on the trade-offs between quality, diversity, and control in hybrid methods, offering a new paradigm for human-in-the-loop design tools.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has combined VAEs with QD, applying MAP-Elites to the latent space of a state-of-the-art diffusion model for PCG is unexplored. This directly merges the high sample quality of deep generative models with the explicit, steerable diversity of QD search.

proposals.title: Co-Adaptive PCG and Learning: A Unified Quality-Diversity Framework for Evolving Game Content and AI Agents
proposals.motivation: In procedural content generation (PCG) for games, content is typically generated first, then a separate AI agent learns to play it. This separation creates a brittle, one-way relationship where the content's difficulty may not match the agent's skill, limiting the potential for adaptive, human-like challenge. Grounded in pre-2026 PCG benchmarks and quality-diversity (QD) optimization, this project addresses the need for systems where content and learning co-evolve to create a continuous, tailored skill curve.
proposals.hypothesis: By co-designing the PCG process and the learning algorithm within a single QD-based optimization loop, we can generate a diverse archive of game levels paired with specialized agent policies that collectively produce a more adaptive and human-like competitive experience than systems where content and learning are separated.
proposals.method: We will extend the MAP-Elites QD algorithm within a 2D game testbed (e.g., a Super Mario Bros. clone). The algorithm will simultaneously evolve a population of game level generators (using latent variable evolution or conditional GANs) and a population of agent policies (e.g., small neural networks). The feature descriptor space will combine level features (e.g., leniency, linearity) and agent behavioral characteristics (e.g., aggression, exploration). The quality function will be a composite of agent performance and a human-likeness score derived from agent trajectory analysis. The system will be benchmarked against a separated PCG-then-learning baseline using the PCG Benchmark framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, unified framework for co-adaptive game design and AI training, a new benchmark task for evaluating such systems, and empirical evidence on the trade-offs between content diversity and agent specialization. It contributes directly to the fields of PCG, game AI, and QD optimization.
proposals.novelty: While QD has been used for PCG and for evolving agent behavior separately, co-evolving them in a single, shared feature space to explicitly shape their interdependence is a novel synthesis. This moves beyond using QD for content *or* agents to using it for the adaptive relationship *between* them.

proposals.title: Generating Social Complexity: A PCG-QD Framework for Multi-Agent Training Scenarios
proposals.motivation: Training robust and generalizable multi-agent systems (MAS) requires exposure to a vast diversity of social and competitive interactions. Current benchmarks and training environments are often limited to static or handcrafted scenarios, which can lead to brittle policies that overfit. Procedural content generation (PCG) offers a solution, but its application to MAS has been underexplored, particularly for generating the nuanced social dynamics that define complex agent interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Quality-Diversity (QD) driven PCG system can automatically generate a broad spectrum of training scenarios that induce diverse, human-like social and competitive behaviors in learning agents, leading to more robust and generalizable multi-agent policies compared to training on fixed or randomly generated environments.
proposals.method: We will build upon the PCG Benchmark framework to create a new testbed for multi-agent social dynamics. Using MAP-Elites or a similar QD algorithm, we will search a space of scenario parameters (e.g., resource distribution, goal structures, communication channels) to generate a repertoire of diverse environments. Behavioral descriptors will be defined using features extracted from agent trajectories during training, such as measures of cooperation, competition, and spatial coordination. Agents will be trained using state-of-the-art multi-agent reinforcement learning (e.g., MADDPG or QMIX) on this dynamically generated curriculum of scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a new open-source benchmark for PCG in multi-agent systems, extending the PCG Benchmark, 2) a methodology for using QD optimization to generate socially complex training curricula, and 3) empirical evidence on how scenario diversity impacts the robustness and generalization of learned multi-agent policies in competitive and partially cooperative games.
proposals.novelty: While PCG and QD have been used for single-agent content and MAS for static environments, their combined application to *generate the social fabric of multi-agent interactions* is novel. This moves beyond generating static levels to dynamically engineering the rules of engagement that shape emergent agent behavior.

proposals.title: Benchmarking and Improving Visual Game Understanding via Quality-Diversity and Human-Like Feature Learning
proposals.motivation: Current game AI benchmarks, including the PCG Benchmark, often rely on symbolic state, which is a poor model for real-world human gameplay. Enhancing the 'visual understanding' track, where agents must play from raw pixels, is crucial for developing more general, human-like AI that can interact with games as humans do. This addresses a core challenge in game AI and provides a more rigorous testbed for algorithms aiming for human-level competence.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating quality-diversity (QD) optimization to generate diverse visual training scenarios and training agents with compute-efficient, human-inspired visual feature extractors, we can significantly improve agent performance and generalization on pixel-based game-playing benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will extend the PCG Benchmark's visual track by using MAP-Elites to generate a diverse archive of game levels that vary in visual complexity and strategic challenge. Agents will be trained using deep reinforcement learning (e.g., PPO or Rainbow DQN) with a novel sensor architecture inspired by 'Human-Like Bots for Tactical Shooters,' focusing on efficient, task-relevant feature extraction from pixels rather than full convolutional stacks. Performance will be evaluated against both existing symbolic agents and human gameplay traces.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a more challenging and nuanced benchmark for visual game understanding, a set of agents with improved generalization and sample efficiency in pixel-based settings, and a methodological framework combining QD and efficient feature learning for game AI. It directly advances the goal of creating more human-competitive game-playing AI.
