proposals.novelty: The novel integration of QD for generating visual training distributions, combined with biologically-plausible, compute-efficient visual sensors tailored for game understanding, moves beyond standard end-to-end CNN approaches and addresses generalization bias in a targeted way.

proposals.title: Meta-RL for Rapid Adaptation to Novel Game Mechanics: A Benchmark for Generalist Game AI
proposals.motivation: Current game-playing AI agents, while superhuman in specific titles, are brittle and fail to transfer skills or adapt to new, unseen game mechanics without extensive retraining. This limits their utility as general game-playing systems and as tools for automated playtesting of novel game designs. A method for fast adaptation would enable more robust and versatile AI for both research and industry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that meta-reinforcement learning (meta-RL) can train a single agent to rapidly adapt its policy to new game mechanics, such as altered physics or novel entity behaviors, with only a few episodes of experience in the new environment, outperforming standard fine-tuning and zero-shot transfer methods.
proposals.method: We will extend the Procedural Content Generation Benchmark (PCG-B) to create a new meta-learning testbed, generating families of game levels that systematically vary core mechanics (e.g., gravity, unit damage types, resource dynamics). We will train a model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML) agent on a distribution of these mechanic variants. The agent's inner-loop adaptation will be evaluated on held-out mechanic sets, measuring sample efficiency and final performance against baselines like multi-task RL and pre-trained networks with fine-tuning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a new, open-source benchmark for evaluating fast adaptation in game AI, 2) a demonstration of meta-RL's efficacy for generalizing across core game mechanics, and 3) a step towards more human-like AI that can learn new rules quickly, with applications in automated game balancing and design.
proposals.novelty: While meta-RL has been applied to level generation and parameter tuning, its targeted use for adapting to fundamental, unseen game mechanics—a core challenge for general AI—within a standardized benchmark is novel. This bridges Togelius's work on PCG benchmarks and quality-diversity with state-of-the-art meta-learning.

proposals.title: Generating a Curriculum of Games: A PCG Benchmark for Progressive Agent Generalization
proposals.motivation: Current game AI agents are typically trained on a single game or a small, fixed set, leading to brittle specialization rather than robust, general skill acquisition. This limits their applicability as testbeds for general AI and their ability to adapt to new, unseen game mechanics. A structured curriculum of games, where complexity increases progressively, could provide a more human-like and effective training paradigm for developing generalist agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training reinforcement learning agents on a procedurally generated curriculum of games, ordered by a learned measure of complexity, will yield agents with superior generalization performance and sample efficiency on novel, held-out games compared to training on a random or fixed set.
proposals.method: We will extend the Procedural Content Generation (PCG) Benchmark framework to generate not just levels, but entire simple 2D game environments with varying mechanics, rules, and objectives. Using Quality-Diversity (QD) algorithms, we will characterize these games across feature dimensions (e.g., action-space branching factor, partial observability, reward sparsity). A curriculum will be constructed by ordering games via these features, and agents (e.g., PPO or IMPALA) will be trained progressively. We will benchmark generalization against a held-out test set of games.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, open-source benchmark for curriculum learning and generalization in game AI, 2) empirical evidence on the effectiveness of game curricula for agent training, and 3) a methodology for automatically characterizing and ordering game complexity for curriculum generation.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning has been used within single games, applying it across a distribution of distinct game *titles* generated via PCG is novel. The key innovation is using QD-based feature analysis to automatically structure the curriculum, moving beyond hand-designed sequences.

proposals.title: Inferring Game Mechanics: A Quality-Diversity Approach to Reverse-Engineering VGDL from Gameplay Traces
proposals.motivation: Procedural Content Generation (PCG) typically moves from rules to content. The inverse problem—inferring rules from gameplay—is a core challenge for understanding player cognition, automating game design, and creating adaptive AI. The Video Game Description Language (VGDL) provides a formal, testable target for this inference, but existing work focuses on forward generation, not reverse-engineering from observation.
proposals.hypothesis: A search-based approach using Quality-Diversity (QD) optimization can efficiently explore the space of possible VGDL rule sets, generating a diverse Pareto front of rule interpretations that explain observed gameplay traces, outperforming single-point optimization methods.
proposals.method: We will treat gameplay traces (agent/human state-action sequences) as input. A QD algorithm (e.g., MAP-Elites) will search the space of VGDL rule components (interaction set, termination conditions). The behavioral descriptor will be a vector of high-level game features (e.g., score progression, entity lifespan distribution) extracted from simulations of candidate rules. Fitness will be the similarity between the simulated and observed gameplay traces, measured via sequence alignment and event matching. The PCG Benchmark framework will be used for evaluation and reproducibility.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel benchmark for the inverse PCG problem within the established PCG Benchmark ecosystem. It will deliver a functional system for VGDL inference and a formalized methodology for evaluating rule recovery, advancing towards AI that can learn game rules through observation like humans do.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses search for PCG, applying QD optimization to the *inverse* problem of rule inference is novel. This approach explicitly seeks multiple viable rule interpretations, capturing the ambiguity inherent in observing gameplay, rather than a single 'correct' solution.

proposals.title: Bootstrapping Game Level Generation with Few-Shot Prompts to Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: Procedural Content Generation (PCG) for games often suffers from limited domain-specific training data, leading to repetitive or low-quality outputs. Meanwhile, large pre-trained vision-language models (VLMs) like CLIP and text-to-image models like DALL-E 2/Stable Diffusion have captured rich semantic and stylistic knowledge from vast non-game corpora. This project investigates how to effectively transfer this general-world knowledge to game-specific generation tasks, enabling high-quality, diverse content creation with minimal game data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by using prompt-based fine-tuning and quality-diversity (QD) optimization as an adapter, pre-trained VLMs can be steered to generate novel, playable, and human-preferred game levels from minimal examples, outperforming models trained solely on small game datasets.
proposals.method: We will use the PCG Benchmark as a testbed, focusing on 2D platformer levels. A pre-trained VLM (e.g., CLIP) will encode level image prompts and target style/textual descriptions. A small decoder network, initialized randomly, will be trained via few-shot learning to reconstruct levels from these embeddings. This system will then be integrated with a QD algorithm (e.g., MAP-Elites) using the VLM's embedding space to define behavioral features, searching for diverse, high-quality levels that satisfy gameplay constraints (e.g., beatability). Human evaluation via benchmarks will assess novelty and playability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for data-efficient PCG via transfer learning, extending the PCG Benchmark with new few-shot challenges. It will yield insights into aligning general-purpose generative models with domain-specific functional constraints, contributing to both game AI and efficient machine learning.
proposals.novelty: While pre-trained models are common, their application to PCG has been limited. The novel integration of prompt-based few-shot learning with QD optimization specifically for steering and diversifying VLM outputs towards functional game content is unexplored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: MAP-Elites with Preference Vectors: Evolving a Repertoire of Partially Observable Strategies for Game AI
proposals.motivation: Quality-Diversity (QD) algorithms like MAP-Elites excel at generating diverse, high-performing solutions in fully observable domains, but their application to partially observable Markov decision processes (POMDPs) remains underexplored. Many real-world and game scenarios, from poker to real-time strategy games with fog of war, require agents to reason under uncertainty and balance competing objectives like aggression and caution. Current methods often optimize for a single reward, lacking a principled way to discover a spectrum of robust, specialized policies for such complex, imperfect-information tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a set of competing preference vectors (e.g., weighting aggression vs. defense, exploration vs. exploitation) into a QD algorithm's objective function, we can evolve a richer, more robust repertoire of policies for POMDPs than by optimizing for a single scalar reward or using standard behavioral descriptors alone.
proposals.method: We will extend MAP-Elites to operate in a multi-objective QD (MO-QD) framework for POMDPs. The policy space will be defined by neural networks (e.g., LSTMs or transformers) processing observation histories. The algorithm's map will use traditional behavioral descriptors (e.g., game-specific statistics), but each cell's quality will be defined by a scalarized combination of multiple objectives (e.g., win rate, damage dealt, resources gathered) using a set of fixed preference vectors. We will benchmark this 'MAP-Elites with Preference Vectors' (MAP-Elites-PV) against single-objective MAP-Elites and Pareto-front methods in PCGRL-like environments and a simplified real-time strategy game simulator with fog of war.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel algorithm for generating diverse, high-performing AI for imperfect-information games and robotics, directly contributing to the PCG benchmark initiative. It will provide a formal framework for studying trade-offs in agent behavior under uncertainty and yield a repertoire of policies that can be selectively deployed or combined for robust performance.
proposals.novelty: While MO-QD exists, its application to POMDPs with preference vectors as a core mechanism for defining behavioral niches in imperfect-information games is novel. This fuses Togelius's interests in game AI benchmarks, human-like behavior generation, and understanding trade-offs via QD into a single, concrete technical approach.

proposals.title: Diverse Policy Discovery in Complex RL Environments via Covariance Matrix Adaptation MAP-Elites
proposals.motivation: Reinforcement learning (RL) often converges to a single high-performing policy, ignoring the vast space of viable, diverse strategies. This is a critical limitation for game AI, where robustness and adaptability require a repertoire of behaviors, and for real-world applications where a single-point failure is unacceptable. Quality-Diversity (QD) algorithms, like MAP-Elites, address this by illuminating the performance landscape, but their efficiency in high-dimensional, complex RL domains remains a challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CMA-ME, which combines the efficient search of Covariance Matrix Adaptation Evolution Strategy (CMA-ES) with the archive-based illumination of MAP-Elites, will discover a broader and higher-performing set of policies in complex, continuous control and game-based RL benchmarks than standard policy gradient methods or naive MAP-Elites.
proposals.method: We will implement CMA-ME to optimize neural network policies for diverse RL environments, including the Procgen Benchmark and MuJoCo continuous control tasks. The behavioral descriptors (features) for the QD archive will be defined as salient, human-interpretable characteristics of agent behavior (e.g., movement patterns, resource usage, tactical positions). We will benchmark CMA-ME against PPO (a standard RL baseline) and MAP-Elites with a simple genetic algorithm, measuring the coverage and maximum fitness of the resulting policy archives.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, empirical evaluation of a leading QD algorithm within mainstream RL, establishing a new benchmark for diverse policy discovery. It will yield open-source, diverse policy archives for game and simulation environments, serving as a resource for robust AI and human-like bot design. The findings will clarify the trade-offs between policy diversity and peak performance in complex domains.
proposals.novelty: While CMA-ME has shown promise in low-dimensional optimization, its application to training deep RL policies in complex, pixel-based or high-DOF environments is novel. This bridges the QD field, focused on illumination, with the RL community's drive for scalable, high-performing agents, moving beyond single-solution paradigms.

proposals.title: Uncertainty-Aware Meta-Reasoning for Safe Exploration in the PCG Benchmark
proposals.motivation: Modern game AI research, including work on the PCG Benchmark, emphasizes agents that can learn and adapt in novel, procedurally generated environments. However, most agents assume a perfect forward model or lack mechanisms to reason about their own model's reliability, leading to catastrophic failures in safety-critical or irreversible game states (e.g., permadeath). This limits their robustness and human-like capacity for cautious exploration in truly unknown settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an agent equipped with explicit, uncertainty-aware meta-reasoning over its learned world model will achieve superior sample efficiency and safety in model-limited games, outperforming standard model-based RL agents by avoiding self-identified high-risk states.
proposals.method: We will extend a model-based RL agent (e.g., DreamerV2) with an ensemble of probabilistic world models to quantify epistemic uncertainty. A meta-reasoning module will map uncertainty estimates to a risk-averse policy bias, modulating the planning horizon or action selection via a quality-diversity optimizer to generate diverse, safe exploration strategies. We will test this agent on custom, safety-critical scenarios within the PCG Benchmark framework, where irreversible actions exist.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, uncertainty-quantified agent architecture for safe exploration in games. It will provide a new benchmark challenge focused on irreversible failure within the PCG Benchmark ecosystem and offer insights into the trade-offs between exploration, model confidence, and catastrophic risk.
proposals.novelty: While uncertainty estimation exists in RL, its principled integration as a meta-reasoning signal for dynamically modulating safety and exploration strategy in game agents is novel. This moves beyond simple risk-aversion to an agent that explicitly reasons about what it does not know.

proposals.title: Generating Robust Game Agents via Model-Free Quality-Diversity and Human-in-the-Loop Benchmarking
proposals.motivation: Most high-performing game-playing agents, from AlphaGo to AlphaStar, rely on a perfect forward model for planning or self-play. This assumption fails in many real-world scenarios, including commercial games with hidden logic, physical simulations with approximation errors, or games under active development. Developing agents that can learn and compete without this perfect simulator is crucial for more general and deployable AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model-free Quality-Diversity (QD) approach, guided by human-like behavioral features and iteratively refined through a human-in-the-loop benchmark, can generate a diverse repertoire of robust agents that effectively compete in games with unknown or imperfect dynamics.
proposals.method: We will extend the MAP-Elites QD algorithm to a model-free setting using policy gradient or evolutionary strategies. Agents will be characterized by a feature descriptor based on pre-2026 'human-like' metrics (e.g., spatial coverage, resource gathering patterns). Training will occur within a new benchmark built on the PCG Benchmark framework, featuring games with deliberately obscured or noisy dynamics (e.g., using the Gymnasium API with wrapped, non-deterministic simulators). Human players will provide demonstrations and pairwise evaluations to shape the reward function and feature space.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel model-free QD method for generating diverse, robust policies, 2) an open-source benchmark for evaluating agents under imperfect forward models, and 3) empirical analysis of the trade-offs between behavioral diversity, robustness, and competitive performance in such environments.
proposals.novelty: While QD has been used for game content generation and analyzing classifier bias, its application to learning robust, competitive agents without a forward model is novel. The integration of human-in-the-loop benchmarking to define 'robustness' and 'human-like' features in this context moves beyond purely simulation-based agent evaluation.

proposals.title: Benchmarking and Generating Human-Like Agents for Continuous Control Games via Quality-Diversity and Latent Prompts
proposals.motivation: While AI excels in discrete action games (e.g., Go, chess), mastering games with continuous, high-dimensional action spaces (e.g., racing, fighting, complex platformers) remains a significant challenge, especially for achieving human-like, adaptive play. This gap limits the use of AI for playtesting, companion bots, and studying complex skill acquisition. A systematic benchmark for generating diverse, human-like agents in such domains is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid approach combining Quality-Diversity (QD) optimization with latent-conditioned policy networks can generate a broad spectrum of high-performing, human-like agents for continuous control games, surpassing the diversity and behavioral authenticity of agents produced by standard reinforcement learning alone.
proposals.method: We will extend the PCG Benchmark framework to include continuous control games like the OpenAI Gym MuJoCo suite and racing simulators. Agent policies will be trained using a MAP-Elites QD algorithm, where the behavioral descriptor space is defined by human-interpretable features (e.g., aggression, caution, style metrics). A variational autoencoder (VAE) will be trained on human gameplay traces to produce a latent 'prompt' space, which will condition a neural network policy, seeding the QD search with human-like priors. Performance will be evaluated against both score-based benchmarks and human player judgments.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a new open-source benchmark for human-like agent generation in continuous control games, 2) a novel method integrating latent behavioral prompts with QD for efficient, diverse policy generation, and 3) new insights into the trade-offs between performance, diversity, and human-likeness in complex action spaces.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in fusing latent representation learning from human data with QD optimization specifically for continuous control, moving beyond level generation or discrete-action bots, to directly address the challenge of generating a population of compelling, human-like opponents and teammates for mechanically complex games.

proposals.title: Active Causal Discovery of Game Mechanics: A Benchmark for Rule Inference via Strategic Intervention
proposals.motivation: Current PCG and AI game-playing benchmarks assume agents have full knowledge of game rules, which is unrealistic for human-like learning and limits AI generality. Drawing from pre-2026 advances in causal discovery and active learning, this research addresses the fundamental challenge of how an AI can autonomously discover the causal rules of a game environment. This is critical for developing more adaptive, sample-efficient, and explainable game AI that can learn from first principles, akin to a human player.
proposals.hypothesis: An AI agent equipped with active causal discovery algorithms can efficiently infer the underlying causal graph of game mechanics through targeted interventions, and this learned model will enable competitive or creative performance comparable to agents with full rule knowledge.
proposals.method: We will develop a benchmark within an existing PCG testbed (e.g., the GVGAI or Procgen framework) where rule sets are hidden. The agent will use a combination of a neural network for state/action representation and a causal structure learning algorithm (e.g., based on the PC algorithm or differentiable causal discovery). An intervention planner, guided by quality-diversity optimization, will generate informative, diverse action sequences to test specific causal hypotheses (e.g., 'does jumping on an enemy cause damage?'). The agent iteratively refines its causal Bayesian network of game dynamics from the observed interventional data.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel benchmark for evaluating AI's causal reasoning and rule inference capabilities in games, 2) a methodology merging active learning and causal discovery for dynamic model building, and 3) empirical results on the trade-offs between exploration for knowledge and exploitation for score, advancing towards more general, human-like game AI.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses games as testbeds for AI, this inverts the paradigm by treating the game itself as an unknown causal system to be discovered, not just a problem to be solved. It explicitly integrates causal inference techniques with strategic intervention planning, a combination not yet standardized in PCG or game AI benchmarks.

proposals.title: Bayesian Rule Inference for Directed Novelty Search in Procedural Game Environments
proposals.motivation: Current AI agents for procedural content generation and game playing assume known or fully observable rules, limiting their applicability to novel or user-generated games where objectives and constraints are latent. This is a critical gap for creating general game-playing agents and for automated game testing, where the system must efficiently discover both how a game works and what is possible within it. A method that unifies rule inference with directed exploration would significantly advance sample-efficient AI for unknown games.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling game rules as latent variables within a Bayesian optimization framework, using an acquisition function that jointly optimizes for rule information gain and behavioral novelty, will yield more sample-efficient and robust exploration in partially observable, procedurally generated game levels compared to standard novelty search or random exploration.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent that maintains a probabilistic model (e.g., a Bayesian network or program synthesis prior) over possible game rules. This model will be updated via interactions with a game environment, such as those from the PCG Benchmark. The agent will use a Quality-Diversity algorithm (e.g., MAP-Elites) to maintain an archive of discovered behaviors. The key innovation is a Bayesian optimization loop where the acquisition function, such as a modified Expected Improvement, will evaluate candidate actions based on their predicted contribution to reducing rule entropy and expanding the novelty of the behavior archive.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a unified framework for directed exploration in rule-unknown environments, directly applicable to automated game testing and general game AI. It will produce a novel benchmark extension for the PCG Benchmark focusing on latent-rule discovery and provide empirical evidence on the trade-offs between exploration for knowledge and for behavioral diversity.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious fusion lies in using Bayesian optimization over a space of hypothesized rules to directly guide a novelty search process, creating a principled, information-theoretic driver for quality-diversity exploration in environments where the objective function itself is unknown.

proposals.title: Generating and Benchmarking Human-Like Agents for Partially Observable Game Environments
proposals.motivation: Most AI game-playing agents and procedural content generation (PCG) benchmarks assume full observability of the game state, which is unrealistic for many real-world games and human-player interactions. This limits the applicability of AI to games with fog-of-war, hidden cards, or limited sensory input, and fails to model key aspects of human-like strategic reasoning under uncertainty. Addressing this gap is crucial for developing more general and human-compatible game AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating partial observability into the PCG benchmark framework and using quality-diversity (QD) optimization, we can generate a diverse population of agents that exhibit human-like strategies (e.g., exploration, information gathering, bluffing) not seen in fully observable settings.
proposals.method: We will extend the open-source PCG Benchmark to support standard partially observable environments (e.g., a simplified real-time strategy fog-of-war scenario or a card game subset). We will train agents using deep reinforcement learning (e.g., recurrent PPO or DQN with LSTM) to handle temporal dependencies. These agents will then be optimized using MAP-Elites, with behavioral descriptors (features) quantifying information-seeking and risk-taking, to generate a diverse archive of strategies. Their performance and behavior will be evaluated against both scripted bots and human player traces.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a new benchmark challenge for PCG and game AI focused on partial observability, 2) a novel application of QD optimization for generating diverse, human-like strategic behaviors under uncertainty, and 3) open-source agent models that serve as more challenging and realistic opponents for human players.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied partial observability in isolation, the novelty lies in explicitly combining it with QD-driven generation of diverse agent strategies within a standardized benchmark, creating a new testbed for evaluating human-like AI and generative methods in imperfect information settings.

proposals.title: Synthesizing General Game-Playing Agents via Program Induction and Quality-Diversity Search
proposals.motivation: Current AI for games is often specialized to a single title or requires extensive retraining, limiting generalizability and interpretability. Program synthesis, which generates human-readable, executable code, offers a path toward more general and transparent game-playing agents. This aligns with the long-standing goal in AI of creating systems that can understand and act upon formal game rules, a core challenge in general game playing (GGP).
proposals.hypothesis: By combining a neural program induction model with a quality-diversity (QD) optimization algorithm, we can synthesize diverse, executable game-playing programs directly from a formal game description, producing strategies that are both high-performing and behaviorally distinct.
proposals.method: We will use the Video Game Description Language (VGDL) as our formal game description framework. A transformer-based encoder will process the VGDL rules and initial game state to produce an embedding. This embedding will condition a program generator, likely based on a differentiable interpreter or a sequence-to-sequence model, to output code in a restricted domain-specific language (DSL) for game actions. This generator will be trained via reinforcement learning, where the reward is game score, and its output will be optimized using MAP-Elites, a QD algorithm, to populate an archive of programs maximizing both performance and a behavioral descriptor (e.g., aggression, resource collection pattern).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel benchmark for strategy generation within the PCG benchmark ecosystem, focusing on the synthesis of interpretable AI. It will advance methods for creating human-like and diverse game-playing bots by generating explicit strategies, moving beyond black-box neural policies. The resulting archive of programs will serve as a tool for analyzing strategic trade-offs in game design.
proposals.novelty: While program synthesis and GGP exist, the integration of quality-diversity search with neural program induction for generating a *population* of distinct, executable game strategies from a formal description is a novel synthesis. This moves beyond optimizing a single policy to creating a diverse toolbox of interpretable AI behaviors.

proposals.title: Inferring Game Mechanics via Inverse Playtrace Analysis: A Benchmark for Rule Reconstruction from Agent Behavior
proposals.motivation: Procedural Content Generation (PCG) and automated game design require formal rule representations, but many games, especially digital ones, have opaque or undocumented mechanics. This creates a barrier for AI research in game understanding and generation. Observing that a successful agent's behavior implicitly encodes a model of the game's rules, we propose to treat rule inference as a core AI challenge, enabling automated analysis of legacy or black-box games.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a combination of sequence modeling and symbolic regression can accurately reconstruct a game's core mechanics and victory conditions from the state-action traces of a high-performing, but not necessarily optimal, AI agent.
proposals.method: We will develop a benchmark within the existing PCG Benchmark framework, creating synthetic game environments with known, variable rule sets. We will train diverse agents (e.g., via reinforcement learning and Monte Carlo Tree Search) to generate playtraces. Our inference model will use a transformer encoder to process state-action sequences, coupled with a neuro-symbolic module that outputs candidate rule predicates (e.g., logical conditions for scoring). Quality-Diversity optimization will be used to generate a population of plausible rule sets, evaluated against the agent's observed behavior.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, open-source benchmark for the inverse problem of game rule inference, 2) a methodology for extracting human-interpretable game rules from agent behavior, and 3) new insights into the relationship between agent policy and the underlying game mechanics, advancing automated game understanding.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes agent behavior for bot detection or skill modeling, the explicit, benchmarked task of inferring *generative game rules* from agent traces is novel. It inverts the standard PCG and game AI pipeline, focusing on explanation over generation or competition.

proposals.title: MetaGen: A Meta-Learning Framework for Cross-Genre Procedural Content Generation
proposals.motivation: Current procedural content generation (PCG) models are typically trained and evaluated on a single game or genre, limiting their generalizability and requiring extensive retraining for new domains. This is inefficient and fails to capture the human-like ability to transfer design principles across different types of games. A model capable of rapid adaptation to new game genres would represent a significant step towards more general and creative AI for game design.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learning model, trained on a diverse corpus of game levels from distinct genres, can learn a latent space of design principles that enables few-shot adaptation to generate competent levels for entirely new game genres, outperforming genre-specific models in adaptation speed and quality.
proposals.method: We will employ a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) framework. The outer loop will train a shared neural network (a Transformer-based generator) on a meta-dataset of levels from multiple genres (e.g., platformers, shooters, puzzles). The inner loop will perform few-shot fine-tuning on a held-out target genre. We will leverage the PCG Benchmark for standardized evaluation and use Quality-Diversity (QD) optimization to ensure the model learns a diverse set of viable design strategies, not just a single solution. Adaptation performance will be measured against genre-specific baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first benchmark for cross-genre PCG meta-learning and an open-source model capable of few-shot adaptation. It will provide empirical evidence on the transferability of design knowledge across games, advancing the field towards more general game AI. The framework will also serve as a testbed for studying human-like creative adaptation in AI systems.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been explored for game-playing agents, its application to cross-genre *content generation* is novel. The integration of QD optimization within the meta-learning loop to explicitly promote a repertoire of adaptable design strategies, rather than a single policy, is a non-obvious extension of existing methods.

ideas.title: AI as a Causal Learner: Inferring Game Dynamics via Active Experimentation
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption that an AI knows the game rules by drawing an analogy to causal discovery in scientific fields. The analogous problem is inferring an unknown causal network from observational data, which is solved through active experimentation and intervention analysis. The adapted research idea designs an AI that must discover game rules not through a given rulebook, but by acting as a causal learner. It strategically intervenes in the game environment (e.g., performing specific, potentially sub-optimal, or novel action sequences) to test hypotheses about which game mechanics cause which outcomes. By building a causal model from the differences these interventions produce, the AI can systematically infer the underlying game dynamics, effectively discovering the rules through a process of active scientific inquiry.

ideas.title: Bayesian Rule Inference for Directed Novelty Search
ideas.description: This research direction develops an AI agent that operates in environments with initially unknown or partially observable rules, treating the rule structure itself as a latent variable to be modeled. The agent employs Bayesian optimization not over a known objective function, but over a space of hypothesized rules and potential behaviors, using an acquisition function that balances the reduction of rule uncertainty with the pursuit of high-novelty or high-reward outcomes. This allows the system to strategically sample interactions that are most informative for refining its world model while also efficiently illuminating the archive of possible behaviors. The result is a unified framework for directed exploration that is both sample-efficient and robust to environments where the game's objectives and constraints must be discovered.

ideas.title: Agents with Uncertainty-Aware Meta-Reasoning for Safe Exploration in Model-Limited Games
ideas.description: This research develops agents that can operate without a perfect forward model by explicitly reasoning about the uncertainty of their learned world model. The agent maintains an internal estimate of model confidence and uses it to modulate its behavior, adopting cautious, exploratory strategies in high-uncertainty states to avoid catastrophic, irreversible actions. This allows for robust learning and planning in complex games where the dynamics are initially unknown or partially observable, ensuring the agent does not fail due to model errors it can self-identify. The core advance is a principled integration of uncertainty quantification into the agent's decision-making loop, creating a self-preserving learning system.

ideas.title: Preference-Aware QD for Partially Observable Environments
ideas.description: This research direction investigates Quality-Diversity (QD) algorithms in partially observable environments where agents must reason about hidden information. The core innovation is to evolve a diverse repertoire of policies that are evaluated not against a single global objective, but against a spectrum of competing quality metrics or preference vectors, such as balancing aggression with caution or exploration with exploitation. This forces the algorithm to discover agents that are not only competent under uncertainty but are also specialized for different, potentially conflicting, behavioral niches defined by the quality objectives. The result is a rich set of robust, adaptable policies suitable for deployment in complex, imperfect-information scenarios like poker, real-world robotics with sensor noise, or strategic games with fog of war.

ideas.title: Safe Rule Discovery via Conjecture Scaffolding and Minimax Regret
ideas.description: This system designs an AI that must discover game rules through interaction but is architected to avoid catastrophic misinterpretation. It maintains a probabilistic scaffold of competing rule conjectures, actively designing interactions to test and eliminate the most dangerous or inconsistent hypotheses. The agent's policy is optimized not for a single best-guess rule set, but to minimize potential regret across all plausible rule models it has not yet ruled out, ensuring robust and cautious behavior. This creates a foundation for safe, sample-efficient rule inference even in environments with deceptive or sparse feedback.

ideas.title: Modular, Interference-Managed Lifelong Learning for General Game Playing
ideas.description: This system addresses catastrophic forgetting and poor transfer by employing a core network with game-conditional modulation, allowing specialized pathways to form for different game families. It uses a meta-learned similarity metric to dynamically populate a structured memory buffer, ensuring rehearsal data minimizes interference while maximizing positive skill transfer. An autonomous curriculum manager prioritizes learning sequences that reinforce shared abstractions (like spatial reasoning or resource management) across games, transforming the agent into a robust, accumulating multi-game learner rather than a fragile one.

ideas.title: Multi-Armed Bandit-Driven Adaptive Optimizer Selection for Quality-Diversity
ideas.description: This research synthesizes bandit-based emitter selection with the integration of diverse state-of-the-art optimizers into QD. A multi-armed bandit algorithm is tasked with a two-level decision: first, selecting an archive cell (or emitter) to activate, and second, selecting which optimizer's specific operator (e.g., from CMA-ES, NES, or gradient-based methods) to deploy for generating offspring within that emitter. This creates a meta-adaptive QD algorithm that learns to match the most effective search strategy to different regions of the behavior space and stages of the search, optimizing the use of diverse, powerful optimizers. The synergy enables a more efficient and robust search by dynamically allocating computational resources to the most promising emitters *and* the most effective variation operators for their current context.

ideas.title: Robust Rule Discovery via Behavioral Invariance
ideas.description: This research direction develops an agent that must operate in an environment with unknown, potentially complex rules. Instead of explicitly modeling all possible rules, the agent's objective is to find a single policy that remains effective (i.e., achieves its goal) across the widest possible range of its own exploratory behaviors and hypothesized rule variations. The core innovation is that the process of minimizing behavioral diversity to find a robust solution becomes the mechanism for inferring the true rules: the policy that works universally must be consistent with the hidden environmental constraints, thereby revealing them. This transforms rule discovery from an explicit modeling task into an optimization for cross-behavioral invariance.

ideas.title: Agents as Quantum Systems: Decision-Making Under State Uncertainty
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of complete game state observability by drawing an analogy from quantum mechanics, where a system's true state is fundamentally hidden. The analogous solution uses density matrices to represent statistical knowledge of a quantum system. Adapted to AI research, we propose creating agents that maintain a 'belief density matrix'—a structured representation of a probabilistic ensemble over possible game states—instead of a single state estimate. The agent's decision-making policy is trained to operate over this density matrix, optimizing for expected outcomes. Actions are treated as operations that transform this belief state, with updates analogous to quantum measurement collapse, providing a novel framework for reasoning and acting under partial observability and hidden information.

ideas.title: Adversarial Belief Manipulation Under Partial Observability
ideas.description: This research develops agents that treat partial observability as a dynamic, adversarial phenomenon rather than a static sensory limitation. The core system trains agents in competitive environments where each agent has a unique sensory footprint, and the primary challenge is to construct an internal model of opponents' evolving beliefs based on what they could have observed. Success is measured by the agent's ability to strategically reveal or conceal information to induce specific, exploitable errors in opponents' world models, turning the opacity of the game state into a tactical weapon. This forces the emergence of robust meta-reasoning about perception itself, moving beyond coping with uncertainty to actively managing the informational landscape.

ideas.title: Online Adaptive Behavior Characterization via Dynamic Feature Discovery
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that the space of possible behaviors for an AI agent must be described using a predefined, static set of human-interpretable descriptors (e.g., a fixed behavioral characterization vector). Drawing an analogy from ecology, where scientists use unsupervised topic modeling on raw animal sensor data to discover behavioral motifs without a pre-existing ethogram, we adapt this solution to reinforcement learning and agent evaluation. The proposed method involves treating an agent's trajectories or state-action sequences as a document stream. An online, adaptive topic modeling or feature discovery algorithm continuously processes this stream to identify, cluster, and label emergent behavioral patterns. These dynamically learned 'behavioral topics' form an adaptive descriptor space that characterizes the agent's policy in real-time, enabling more flexible and grounded behavior-driven learning, exploration, and analysis without reliance on pre-specified behavioral axes.

ideas.title: CMA-ME with Learned Behavioral Embeddings for Scalable High-Dimensional Exploration
ideas.description: This research addresses the core scalability flaw of CMA-ME by replacing its fixed, hand-designed behavior characterization with an online-learned, low-dimensional behavioral embedding. A deep embedding model is trained concurrently with the search process to project high-dimensional solutions into a compact space that captures task-relevant behavioral features. The archive is constructed in this learned embedding space, making novelty detection and archive management efficient regardless of parameter count, while CMA-ES subroutines perform local search in the full parameter space. This approach maintains the algorithm's exploratory power while making it viable for domains with hundreds or thousands of continuous parameters.

ideas.title: Bandit-Meta-Learned Adaptive Acquisition for Robust Archive Illumination
ideas.description: This approach enhances Bayesian optimization for archive illumination by framing the choice of acquisition function as a non-stationary multi-armed bandit problem. A meta-learner continuously evaluates the performance of a portfolio of diverse acquisition strategies, measuring their success in adding novel and high-quality solutions to the archive. The system then allocates the sampling budget proportionally to the strategies demonstrating recent efficacy, enabling adaptive robustness against deceptive landscapes or sparse feedback. This results in more reliable and efficient exploration of the search space for quality-diversity optimization.

ideas.title: Co-evolutionary Synergy of Pareto-Optimization and Quality-Diversity via Informed Migration
ideas.description: This approach resolves the intrinsic conflict between convergence and divergence by employing two co-evolving populations: a Pareto population optimizing for the multi-objective front and a QD population exploring a defined behavior space. The populations evolve independently with their own selection and archiving mechanisms, eliminating direct selection pressure conflict. A critical, informed migration protocol periodically transfers non-dominated solutions from the Pareto front to the QD archive (as high-quality seeds for new behaviors) and transfers elite, behaviorally novel solutions from the QD archive to the Pareto population (as diverse genetic material to improve convergence and spread). This creates a robust, synergistic system where each component aids the other, enhancing both the quality of the Pareto front and the depth of behavioral exploration.

ideas.title: Archive-Conditioned Trust Regions for Decentralized QD Search
ideas.description: This method maintains a population of local trust regions, each anchored to an elite solution in the QD archive. The adaptation rule for each trust region is conditioned on both the local performance landscape and the density of the archive in its surrounding behavioral space, allowing regions in sparse areas to expand for exploration and regions in crowded, high-performing areas to shrink for exploitation. A meta-controller periodically spawns new trust regions in unexplored behavioral territories and prunes redundant ones, ensuring sustained diversity. This results in a robust, decentralized search strategy that explicitly balances the dual objectives of QD search within the trust region framework.

ideas.title: Bayesian Quality-Diversity Search under Uncertain or Evolving Multi-Objective Preferences
ideas.description: This approach tackles the fundamental instability of QD search with multiple or preference-based quality objectives by formalizing the uncertainty. A probabilistic model, such as a Bayesian linear model or a Gaussian process, infers a distribution over possible scalarization functions or utility models from observed preferences or objective evaluations. The QD algorithm uses this model to compute an acquisition function (e.g., expected improvement or upper confidence bound) for each candidate solution, driving the search toward behaviors that are high-quality across the distribution of likely objectives. This results in a robust archive that is performant and diverse even as our understanding of the 'true' quality objective evolves or remains partially ambiguous.

ideas.title: Apply CMA-ME with Adaptive Archive Pruning for RL Policy Libraries
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from evolutionary biology/phylogenetics to enhance CMA-ME for reinforcement learning domains. In phylogenetics, constructing a tree of life from genetic data requires intelligent pruning of candidate evolutionary histories to manage complexity and focus on distinct, plausible branches. The analogous solution involves algorithms that adaptively prune based on criteria like parsimony. Adapted to the RL context, we propose integrating an adaptive archive pruning mechanism into CMA-ME. This mechanism would dynamically manage the archive by prioritizing the retention of policies that are both high-performing and maximally novel in their behavioral characteristics, actively pruning redundant or low-contribution policies. This approach aims to make the discovery of diverse, high-performing policies in complex environments more computationally efficient, yielding a condensed yet comprehensive library of distinct strategies.

ideas.title: Bayesian Optimization for Noisy Archive Illumination
ideas.description: This research direction develops a Bayesian optimization framework for quality-diversity search in domains with highly stochastic or noisy fitness functions, such as robotics hardware or real-world experiments. It integrates a probabilistic surrogate model, informed by the archive's history, to predict both the expected fitness and the uncertainty (noise) of candidate solutions. The algorithm then strategically decides, for each sampling step, whether to perform a costly replication to reduce noise on a promising incumbent or to expend an evaluation to explore a novel, uncertain region of the behavior space. This enables sample-efficient and reliable archive illumination where direct, repeated fitness evaluations are prohibitively expensive or impractical.

ideas.title: Adapting Clinical Trial Adaptive Randomization for Emitter Selection
ideas.description: This idea applies the principle of adaptive randomization from clinical trial design to the problem of dynamically selecting which archive cell's emitter to activate. In clinical trials, adaptive algorithms like Thompson Sampling are used to allocate more participants to the most promising drug arms based on accumulating outcome data. Analogously, we can treat each emitter as a 'treatment arm' and a relevant performance metric (e.g., data throughput, link stability) as the trial 'outcome.' By implementing a multi-armed bandit algorithm within this framework, the system can continuously learn from the rewards (performance results) of each activation, dynamically shifting its probability of selection towards higher-performing emitters. This balances the need to explore all available options with the goal of maximizing cumulative system performance over time.

ideas.title: CMA-ME with Distribution-Aware Robust Optimization for Stochastic Environments
ideas.description: This approach extends CMA-ME for domains where fitness is not just noisy but stems from a complex, potentially heavy-tailed distribution. Instead of seeking deterministic evaluations, it maintains an online probabilistic model of the performance distribution for each behavior descriptor cell, updated with all rollouts. The search optimizes a robust objective, such as a lower confidence bound or CVaR, computed from these distributions, ensuring discovered solutions are reliably good. Furthermore, an acquisition function balances exploring uncertain performance estimates with exploiting high-quality regions, making the archive robust to the inherent randomness and risk of the domain.

ideas.title: Evolutionary Canalization for Robust Single-Solution Learning
ideas.description: This idea adapts the evolutionary developmental biology concept of 'canalization'—where organisms evolve to produce consistent phenotypes despite genetic or environmental noise—to the challenge of finding a single robust solution in machine learning. The analogous solution involves stabilizing selection and redundant regulatory networks that suppress variation. Adapted to the research context, we propose a training framework that actively minimizes the diversity of a single model's behavioral outputs across a defined space of variations (e.g., data subsets, initialization seeds). By penalizing behavioral divergence and reinforcing convergent pathways, the algorithm canalizes the learning process, ensuring it reliably arrives at one solution that is intrinsically robust to the specified perturbations, thereby achieving the 'Dual' objective of minimizing diversity for robustness.

ideas.title: Council: Diverse Ensemble for Certified Robustness Across Behavior Space
ideas.description: This approach reframes the search for robustness by explicitly maintaining strategic diversity throughout optimization. Instead of collapsing to a single solution, it trains a small set of models that are individually robust but strategically different in their failure modes, formalized through measures like predictive disagreement under perturbation. The final robust output is this ensemble 'council,' where a consensus mechanism or a learned selector provides a decision that is provably more stable and higher in worst-case performance than any single model could achieve alone, effectively distributing risk across the behavior space.

ideas.title: Massively Parallel Robust CMA-ME for Single-Solution Reliability
ideas.description: This research fuses massively parallel CMA-ME with a dual-objective for robustness to create a framework for finding a single, reliable solution to expensive black-box problems. It scales to thousands of parallel workers, each evaluating a candidate solution not just for performance but also for its sensitivity to perturbations within a local region of behavior space. The algorithm's archive and selection mechanisms are adapted to reward and steer the population toward regions that minimize this sensitivity (maximize robustness) while maintaining high objective performance. The result is a method that leverages extreme parallelism to directly and efficiently map the robustness landscape, converging on a final solution guaranteed to be performant and stable against real-world variations.

ideas.title: Hybrid QD-Gradient Search via Basin Hopping Adaptation
ideas.description: This idea hybridizes QD archives with gradient-based local search by drawing an analogy from computational chemistry's Basin-Hopping algorithm. The analogous solution combines stochastic global exploration with deterministic local gradient minimization to efficiently find low-energy molecular states. Adapted to QD, the proposed method operates as follows: when the QD algorithm's variation operators (e.g., mutation, crossover) produce a new candidate, a gradient-based local optimization (e.g., a few steps of gradient ascent/descent) is applied to it, provided the objective function is differentiable. The resulting locally optimized solution is then evaluated for its performance and behavior descriptor, and this refined point is considered for insertion into the archive. This approach marries the broad, exploratory power of QD search with the precision and efficiency of gradient methods, ensuring the archive contains high-quality, locally optimal solutions and that the search efficiently hops between the basins of attraction in the behavior-performance landscape.

ideas.title: Adapting Dimensionality Reduction Techniques from Astrophysics for High-Dimensional CMA-ME
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from astrophysics, where analyzing high-dimensional datasets (like galaxy surveys) is made tractable by focusing on physically meaningful, lower-dimensional subspaces. The analogous solution involves identifying interpretable projections (e.g., the Fundamental Plane) rather than applying methods to the full raw dimensionality. Adapted to CMA-ME, the idea is to scale the algorithm to high-dimensional continuous domains by integrating a dimensionality reduction or manifold learning step. A model (e.g., an autoencoder or simple linear projection) would be trained to identify a lower-dimensional latent space that captures the salient features for behavioral diversity. CMA-ME's search and archive management would then be performed within this latent space, while a decoder maps points back to the original high-dimensional parameter space for evaluation. This approach aims to preserve CMA-ME's exploratory power while making it computationally feasible for benchmarks with significantly more dimensions, such as robotic control tasks with many degrees of freedom or neural network weight optimization.

ideas.title: Hierarchical, Massively Parallel CMA-ME for Modular Design Co-Optimization
ideas.description: This fused approach combines a hierarchical search structure with massive parallelization to tackle the co-design of complex modular systems, such as robotic morphologies or neural network architectures. The hierarchy mirrors the system's modularity, with high-level managers exploring compositions of modules and low-level workers optimizing each module's internal parameters via distributed CMA-ME. Thousands of parallel evaluations are strategically allocated across the hierarchy, allowing concurrent exploration of architectural choices and their detailed instantiation. This synergy makes it feasible to discover high-performing, novel designs in vast, structured search spaces where function evaluations are extremely costly.

ideas.title: Adapting Ensemble Methods from Weather Prediction to Create Hybrid QD Optimizers
ideas.description: This idea proposes to augment Quality-Diversity (QD) algorithms by integrating operators from multiple state-of-the-art optimizers simultaneously, inspired by ensemble methods in Numerical Weather Prediction. In NWP, combining forecasts from multiple models with different core algorithms yields more accurate and robust predictions than any single model. Adapted to QD research, this suggests creating a hybrid optimizer framework where an ensemble of distinct optimizers (e.g., CMA-ES, Natural Evolution Strategies, gradient-based methods) operates within the QD loop. Their proposals would be combined or selectively used per niche, leveraging the diverse search characteristics of each optimizer to improve robustness, convergence speed, and coverage across the behavior space, moving beyond the current paradigm of integrating a single alternative optimizer.

ideas.title: Grammar-Inducing CMA-ME for Adaptive Modular Discovery
ideas.description: This approach integrates a structure-learning component with CMA-ME to autonomously discover a hierarchical, modular representation of the design space. It begins with a flat representation and uses statistical dependencies between solution components and their performance in the archive to iteratively infer a probabilistic context-free grammar. This learned grammar then defines the adaptive hierarchical structure for subsequent rounds of CMA-ME search, enabling the algorithm to automatically identify and exploit the most productive compositional building blocks and their relationships without prior human specification.

ideas.title: Adaptive Memory Archive via Hippocampal Replay and Neurogenesis Analogy
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a static, grid-based archive by drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience, specifically the mammalian hippocampal memory system. The analogous solution involves hippocampal replay for memory consolidation and adult neurogenesis for adaptive memory capacity. Adapted to evolutionary computation, the research idea proposes a dynamic archive that grows strategically ('neurogenesis') to cover new or promising areas of the search space and employs a consolidation mechanism ('replay') to actively reorganize and refine the archive's topology. This creates a self-adaptive archive structure that balances the retention of high-quality solutions with the flexibility to expand and reshape itself based on the ongoing exploration of the problem landscape, moving beyond a fixed, pre-defined grid.

ideas.title: Noise-Robust CMA-ME with Dynamic, Resolution-Adaptive Archives
ideas.description: This research direction fuses techniques for handling noisy fitness evaluations with dynamic archive structures to create a new variant of CMA-ME. The core idea is to couple the uncertainty in fitness evaluations—derived from statistical estimates or repeated sampling—with the decision to locally refine, coarsen, or morph the archive's topology. In regions of high noise or uncertainty, the archive can dynamically increase its resolution or adapt its cell boundaries to better disentangle true performance differences and guide exploration more reliably. Conversely, in confident, well-explored regions, the archive can maintain a coarser representation, making the algorithm robust to noise while being resource-efficient and avoiding the pitfalls of a fixed, pre-defined grid.

ideas.title: Gradient-Aware Diversity Preservation via Directional Repulsion in QD Archives
ideas.description: This redesigned approach hybridizes QD search with gradient information in a diversity-preserving manner. Instead of using gradients for straightforward local ascent, it computes solution gradients to measure directional alignment in objective improvement. A repulsive force is incorporated into the archive update rule, discouraging the convergence of multiple solutions onto the same local optimization pathway. This allows the archive to leverage gradient efficiency for local refinement while actively maintaining and exploring diverse behavioral niches, preventing collapse into a single high-fitness region.

ideas.title: Anchored Online Manifold Learning for Stable Behavior Characterization
ideas.description: This method maintains a fixed, interpretable core behavior descriptor space (e.g., fundamental dynamics) to ensure stability. Online, it learns an adaptive, nonlinear mapping from this core space to a richer, task-optimized behavior characterization manifold. The learning process is constrained by information bottlenecks and alignment penalties to prevent catastrophic distributional shift. A separate validation module continuously monitors the utility and consistency of the learned manifold, allowing for safe reversion to the core descriptors if needed, ensuring robust policy improvement without collapse.

ideas.title: Adapting Portfolio Diversification for Pareto-QD Hybridization
ideas.description: This idea blends QD archiving with Pareto-based selection by drawing an analogy from Financial Portfolio Theory. The analogous solution is Modern Portfolio Theory, which identifies the efficient frontier (Pareto set) of risk and return while mandating diversification across uncorrelated assets to build a robust portfolio. Adapted to evolutionary multi-objective optimization, the method maintains a dual-archive system: a primary archive for the Pareto-optimal front and a secondary quality-diversity archive that maps and fills niches in a behavioral space. Selection and reproduction are guided by a hybrid fitness function that rewards both Pareto dominance and novelty/coverage within the QD archive, ensuring the final solution set is both optimal and diverse in strategy.

ideas.title: Hybrid QD Archives via Ecological Niche Construction Theory
ideas.description: This idea explores hybrid Quality-Diversity (QD) archives by drawing an analogy from community ecology's niche construction theory. In ecology, organisms adapt to their environment while also modifying it to create new niches for others, boosting overall diversity. The analogous solution is this dynamic, reciprocal process of adaptation and environmental modification. Adapted to QD research, the idea proposes a hybrid archive system where CMA-ME's adaptive search capabilities act as 'niche constructors.' These CMA-ME processes would not only search for solutions but also dynamically influence the structure and content of a shared archive (e.g., a CVT or grid archive). This archive would simultaneously be maintained and accessed by other, potentially simpler, QD algorithms for diversity preservation. The result is a reciprocal framework: CMA-ME's adaptation constructs and refines the archive 'environment,' while the stable, diverse archive structure guides and fuels further adaptive exploration, potentially leading to more robust and efficient discovery of diverse, high-performing solutions.

ideas.title: Unified Bayesian Optimization for Mixed Continuous, Discrete, and Categorical Spaces
ideas.description: This research develops a robust hybrid optimization system that natively handles mixed-variable parameter spaces containing continuous, integer, ordinal, and categorical dimensions. It integrates specialized probabilistic surrogate models capable of learning correlations across different variable types into a coherent Bayesian optimization loop. The acquisition function is designed to efficiently trade off exploration and exploitation across the entire heterogeneous space, enabling direct optimization of complex real-world problems like hardware design and molecular discovery. This approach overcomes the fundamental limitation of methods restricted to continuous relaxations by respecting the intrinsic structure of each variable type.

ideas.title: Massively Parallel CMA-ME for High-Dimensional, Multi-Fidelity Real-World Design
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a massively parallel, distributed implementation of the CMA-ME algorithm specifically tailored for high-dimensional, expensive real-world design problems like aerodynamic shapes or material composites. The key innovation is integrating a multi-fidelity evaluation strategy within the distributed architecture, allowing the algorithm to dynamically allocate thousands of parallel workers across cheap proxies and high-cost accurate models to efficiently map the trade-offs and constraints of complex design spaces. This enables the practical discovery of a rich archive of high-performing, diverse designs for problems previously intractable due to their combination of dimensionality, expense, and real-world complexity, moving beyond benchmarks to impactful engineering applications.
